title: Docker Compose Stack for Instant Private AI Deployment
content:
This runbook provides a complete, production-ready Docker Compose stack for deploying a private AI infrastructure with Ollama, ruvector PostgreSQL, Open WebUI, and Nginx reverse proxy.

## Prerequisites
- Docker Engine 24.0+ and Docker Compose v2.20+
- 16GB RAM minimum (32GB recommended for 13B+ models)
- NVIDIA GPU optional (CPU fallback included)
- Domain name with DNS pointing to your server

## File Structure
```
private-ai/
  docker-compose.yml
  docker-compose.gpu.yml
  nginx/
    nginx.conf
    ssl/
  .env
  data/
    ollama/
    postgres/
    webui/
```

## docker-compose.yml
```yaml
version: '3.8'

services:
  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    container_name: ollama
    restart: unless-stopped
    volumes:
      - ollama_data:/root/.ollama
    environment:
      - OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0
      - OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=24h
      - OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=2
    ports:
      - "127.0.0.1:11434:11434"
    networks:
      - ai_internal
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:11434/api/tags"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 60s
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 16G

  ruvector-postgres:
    image: pgvector/pgvector:pg16
    container_name: ruvector-postgres
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_USER=${POSTGRES_USER}
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}
      - POSTGRES_DB=${POSTGRES_DB}
      - PGDATA=/var/lib/postgresql/data/pgdata
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./init-scripts:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d
    ports:
      - "127.0.0.1:5435:5432"
    networks:
      - ai_internal
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U ${POSTGRES_USER} -d ${POSTGRES_DB}"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  open-webui:
    image: ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:latest
    container_name: open-webui
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      ollama:
        condition: service_healthy
    environment:
      - OLLAMA_BASE_URL=http://ollama:11434
      - WEBUI_SECRET_KEY=${WEBUI_SECRET_KEY}
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://${POSTGRES_USER}:${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}@ruvector-postgres:5432/${POSTGRES_DB}
      - ENABLE_SIGNUP=${ENABLE_SIGNUP:-false}
      - DEFAULT_USER_ROLE=user
      - WEBUI_AUTH=true
    volumes:
      - webui_data:/app/backend/data
    networks:
      - ai_internal
      - ai_frontend
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8080/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3

  nginx:
    image: nginx:alpine
    container_name: nginx-proxy
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      - open-webui
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    volumes:
      - ./nginx/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro
      - ./nginx/ssl:/etc/nginx/ssl:ro
      - nginx_logs:/var/log/nginx
    networks:
      - ai_frontend

volumes:
  ollama_data:
    driver: local
    driver_opts:
      type: none
      o: bind
      device: ./data/ollama
  postgres_data:
    driver: local
  webui_data:
    driver: local
  nginx_logs:
    driver: local

networks:
  ai_internal:
    driver: bridge
    internal: true
  ai_frontend:
    driver: bridge
```

## GPU Override: docker-compose.gpu.yml
```yaml
services:
  ollama:
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: 1
              capabilities: [gpu]
```

## .env Template
```bash
POSTGRES_USER=ruvector
POSTGRES_PASSWORD=changeme_strong_password_here
POSTGRES_DB=ruvector_kb
WEBUI_SECRET_KEY=changeme_32char_random_string_here
ENABLE_SIGNUP=false
DOMAIN=ai.yourcompany.internal
```

## Nginx Configuration (nginx/nginx.conf)
```nginx
events { worker_connections 1024; }
http {
  upstream webui { server open-webui:8080; }
  server {
    listen 80;
    server_name ${DOMAIN};
    return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
  }
  server {
    listen 443 ssl http2;
    server_name ${DOMAIN};
    ssl_certificate /etc/nginx/ssl/fullchain.pem;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/nginx/ssl/privkey.pem;
    ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;
    ssl_ciphers HIGH:!aNULL:!MD5;
    client_max_body_size 100M;
    location / {
      proxy_pass http://webui;
      proxy_http_version 1.1;
      proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
      proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
      proxy_set_header Host $host;
      proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
      proxy_read_timeout 300s;
    }
  }
}
```

## Deployment Commands
```bash
# CPU-only deployment
docker compose up -d

# GPU deployment
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.gpu.yml up -d

# Pull initial model
docker exec ollama ollama pull llama3.2:3b
docker exec ollama ollama pull nomic-embed-text

# Check health
docker compose ps
docker compose logs -f ollama
```

## Post-Deployment Verification
```bash
# Verify Ollama is responding
curl http://localhost:11434/api/tags

# Verify PostgreSQL with pgvector
docker exec ruvector-postgres psql -U ruvector -d ruvector_kb -c "SELECT extversion FROM pg_extension WHERE extname='vector';"

# Test embedding generation
curl http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings -d '{"model":"nomic-embed-text","prompt":"test"}'
```

title: Kubernetes Helm Deployment for Production Private AI
content:
This runbook covers deploying a production-grade private AI stack on Kubernetes using Helm, with GPU node affinity, autoscaling, and persistent storage.

## Prerequisites
- Kubernetes 1.28+
- Helm 3.12+
- NVIDIA GPU Operator installed (for GPU nodes)
- Storage class with ReadWriteOnce support
- cert-manager for TLS

## Namespace and RBAC Setup
```bash
kubectl create namespace private-ai
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=private-ai
```

## Ollama Deployment (ollama-deployment.yaml)
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ollama
  namespace: private-ai
  labels:
    app: ollama
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ollama
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ollama
    spec:
      nodeSelector:
        gpu-type: nvidia
      tolerations:
        - key: nvidia.com/gpu
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
        - name: ollama
          image: ollama/ollama:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 11434
          env:
            - name: OLLAMA_HOST
              value: "0.0.0.0"
            - name: OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE
              value: "24h"
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "8Gi"
              cpu: "2"
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
            limits:
              memory: "24Gi"
              cpu: "8"
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
          volumeMounts:
            - name: ollama-storage
              mountPath: /root/.ollama
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /api/tags
              port: 11434
            initialDelaySeconds: 60
            periodSeconds: 30
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /api/tags
              port: 11434
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10
      volumes:
        - name: ollama-storage
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: ollama-pvc
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: ollama-pvc
  namespace: private-ai
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  storageClassName: fast-ssd
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 100Gi
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: ollama
  namespace: private-ai
spec:
  selector:
    app: ollama
  ports:
    - port: 11434
      targetPort: 11434
  type: ClusterIP
```

## HorizontalPodAutoscaler (for CPU-only Ollama replicas)
```yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: ollama-cpu-hpa
  namespace: private-ai
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: ollama-cpu
  minReplicas: 1
  maxReplicas: 5
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: memory
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 80
```

## ruvector PostgreSQL StatefulSet
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: ruvector-postgres
  namespace: private-ai
spec:
  serviceName: ruvector-postgres
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ruvector-postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ruvector-postgres
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: postgres
          image: pgvector/pgvector:pg16
          env:
            - name: POSTGRES_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-secret
                  key: username
            - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-secret
                  key: password
            - name: POSTGRES_DB
              value: ruvector_kb
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "2Gi"
              cpu: "500m"
            limits:
              memory: "8Gi"
              cpu: "2"
          volumeMounts:
            - name: postgres-data
              mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: postgres-data
      spec:
        accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
        storageClassName: fast-ssd
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: 50Gi
```

## Ingress with TLS
```yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: private-ai-ingress
  namespace: private-ai
  annotations:
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-internal
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size: "100m"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-read-timeout: "300"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - ai.internal.company.com
      secretName: private-ai-tls
  rules:
    - host: ai.internal.company.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: open-webui
                port:
                  number: 8080
```

## Secrets Management
```bash
# Create secrets (never store in git)
kubectl create secret generic postgres-secret \
  --from-literal=username=ruvector \
  --from-literal=password=$(openssl rand -base64 32) \
  --namespace=private-ai

kubectl create secret generic webui-secret \
  --from-literal=secret-key=$(openssl rand -base64 32) \
  --namespace=private-ai
```

## Helm Values Override (values-production.yaml)
```yaml
global:
  storageClass: fast-ssd
  namespace: private-ai

ollama:
  replicaCount: 1
  gpu:
    enabled: true
    count: 1
  persistence:
    size: 100Gi
  models:
    - llama3.2:3b
    - nomic-embed-text

postgres:
  persistence:
    size: 50Gi
  backup:
    enabled: true
    schedule: "0 2 * * *"
```

title: Air-Gapped Installation Runbook for Private AI
content:
This runbook covers deploying a complete private AI stack in environments with no internet access, including pre-downloading artifacts, setting up a local container registry, and distributing models within the air-gapped network.

## Phase 1: Prepare Artifacts on Internet-Connected Machine

### 1.1 Pull and Save Docker Images
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Run on internet-connected machine
SAVE_DIR="/tmp/airgap-artifacts"
mkdir -p $SAVE_DIR/images

IMAGES=(
  "ollama/ollama:latest"
  "pgvector/pgvector:pg16"
  "ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:latest"
  "nginx:alpine"
  "prom/prometheus:latest"
  "grafana/grafana:latest"
  "registry:2"
)

for img in "${IMAGES[@]}"; do
  echo "Pulling: $img"
  docker pull $img
  filename=$(echo $img | tr '/:' '_')
  docker save $img | gzip > $SAVE_DIR/images/${filename}.tar.gz
done

echo "Images saved to $SAVE_DIR/images/"
ls -lh $SAVE_DIR/images/
```

### 1.2 Download Ollama Models
```bash
# Pull models to local directory
mkdir -p $SAVE_DIR/models

# Start temporary Ollama to download
docker run -d --name ollama-temp \
  -v $SAVE_DIR/models:/root/.ollama \
  ollama/ollama:latest

# Download models
MODELS=("llama3.2:3b" "llama3.1:8b" "nomic-embed-text" "mistral:7b")
for model in "${MODELS[@]}"; do
  echo "Downloading: $model"
  docker exec ollama-temp ollama pull $model
done

docker stop ollama-temp && docker rm ollama-temp

echo "Models saved. Size:"
du -sh $SAVE_DIR/models/
```

### 1.3 Bundle All Artifacts
```bash
# Create checksums for integrity verification
cd $SAVE_DIR
find . -type f -name '*.tar.gz' | xargs sha256sum > checksums.sha256
find ./models -type f | xargs sha256sum >> checksums.sha256

# Create transfer bundle (split for large files)
tar czf - . | split -b 4G - airgap-bundle.tar.gz.

echo "Transfer these files to air-gapped environment:"
ls -lh airgap-bundle.tar.gz.*
```

## Phase 2: Set Up Local Container Registry in Air-Gapped Environment

### 2.1 Bootstrap Registry
```bash
# On air-gapped host - load registry image first
gunzip -c images/registry_2.tar.gz | docker load

# Start local registry
docker run -d \
  --name local-registry \
  --restart=unless-stopped \
  -p 5000:5000 \
  -v /data/registry:/var/lib/registry \
  registry:2

echo "Registry running at localhost:5000"
```

### 2.2 Load and Push All Images
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Load all saved images and push to local registry
REGISTRY="localhost:5000"

declare -A IMAGE_MAP=(
  ["ollama_ollama_latest.tar.gz"]="ollama/ollama:latest"
  ["pgvector_pgvector_pg16.tar.gz"]="pgvector/pgvector:pg16"
  ["ghcr.io_open-webui_open-webui_latest.tar.gz"]="open-webui:latest"
  ["nginx_alpine.tar.gz"]="nginx:alpine"
  ["prom_prometheus_latest.tar.gz"]="prometheus:latest"
  ["grafana_grafana_latest.tar.gz"]="grafana:latest"
)

for file in "${!IMAGE_MAP[@]}"; do
  original="${IMAGE_MAP[$file]}"
  new_tag="$REGISTRY/$original"
  
  echo "Loading $file..."
  gunzip -c images/$file | docker load
  
  docker tag $original $new_tag
  docker push $new_tag
  echo "Pushed: $new_tag"
done
```

### 2.3 Update docker-compose.yml for Air-Gapped
```bash
# Replace public image references with local registry
sed -i 's|ollama/ollama:latest|localhost:5000/ollama/ollama:latest|g' docker-compose.yml
sed -i 's|pgvector/pgvector:pg16|localhost:5000/pgvector/pgvector:pg16|g' docker-compose.yml
sed -i 's|ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:latest|localhost:5000/open-webui:latest|g' docker-compose.yml
```

## Phase 3: Model Distribution Within Air-Gapped Network

### 3.1 Set Up Model File Server
```bash
# Serve models via HTTP for distribution to multiple nodes
docker run -d \
  --name model-server \
  --restart=unless-stopped \
  -p 8888:80 \
  -v /data/airgap/models:/usr/share/nginx/html:ro \
  localhost:5000/nginx:alpine

echo "Models available at http://$(hostname -I | awk '{print $1}'):8888"
```

### 3.2 Configure Ollama to Use Pre-Downloaded Models
```bash
# Copy models to Ollama data directory
cp -r /data/airgap/models/* /data/ollama/

# Set permissions
chown -R 1000:1000 /data/ollama/

# Verify models are available
docker exec ollama ollama list
```

## Phase 4: Verification Checklist
```bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Air-Gap Verification ==="

# 1. No outbound connections
echo -n "Checking for outbound internet: "
curl -s --max-time 5 https://google.com > /dev/null 2>&1 && echo "FAIL - internet reachable" || echo "PASS - no internet"

# 2. Registry accessible
echo -n "Local registry: "
curl -s http://localhost:5000/v2/_catalog | grep -q repositories && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"

# 3. Ollama running
echo -n "Ollama API: "
curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/tags | grep -q models && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"

# 4. Models loaded
echo "Loaded models:"
docker exec ollama ollama list

# 5. Vector DB
echo -n "PostgreSQL pgvector: "
docker exec ruvector-postgres psql -U ruvector -d ruvector_kb -c "SELECT 1" > /dev/null 2>&1 && echo "PASS" || echo "FAIL"
```

title: Monitoring and Alerting for Local LLM Infrastructure
content:
Complete observability stack for private AI deployments using Prometheus, Grafana, and Loki. This runbook covers metrics collection, dashboard configuration, alerting rules, and log aggregation.

## Architecture Overview
- Prometheus scrapes metrics from Ollama and system exporters
- Grafana provides dashboards for LLM-specific metrics
- Loki aggregates logs from all containers
- Alertmanager routes alerts to Slack/email/PagerDuty

## docker-compose.monitoring.yml
```yaml
version: '3.8'

services:
  prometheus:
    image: prom/prometheus:latest
    container_name: prometheus
    restart: unless-stopped
    command:
      - '--config.file=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml'
      - '--storage.tsdb.retention.time=30d'
      - '--storage.tsdb.path=/prometheus'
      - '--web.enable-lifecycle'
    volumes:
      - ./monitoring/prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml:ro
      - ./monitoring/alerts:/etc/prometheus/alerts:ro
      - prometheus_data:/prometheus
    ports:
      - "127.0.0.1:9090:9090"
    networks:
      - ai_internal

  grafana:
    image: grafana/grafana:latest
    container_name: grafana
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD=${GRAFANA_PASSWORD}
      - GF_SERVER_ROOT_URL=https://${DOMAIN}/grafana
      - GF_SMTP_ENABLED=true
      - GF_SMTP_HOST=${SMTP_HOST}
      - GF_AUTH_ANONYMOUS_ENABLED=false
    volumes:
      - grafana_data:/var/lib/grafana
      - ./monitoring/dashboards:/etc/grafana/provisioning/dashboards:ro
      - ./monitoring/datasources:/etc/grafana/provisioning/datasources:ro
    networks:
      - ai_internal

  loki:
    image: grafana/loki:latest
    container_name: loki
    restart: unless-stopped
    command: -config.file=/etc/loki/config.yaml
    volumes:
      - ./monitoring/loki-config.yaml:/etc/loki/config.yaml:ro
      - loki_data:/loki
    networks:
      - ai_internal

  promtail:
    image: grafana/promtail:latest
    container_name: promtail
    restart: unless-stopped
    volumes:
      - /var/log:/var/log:ro
      - /var/lib/docker/containers:/var/lib/docker/containers:ro
      - ./monitoring/promtail-config.yaml:/etc/promtail/config.yaml:ro
    networks:
      - ai_internal

  node-exporter:
    image: prom/node-exporter:latest
    container_name: node-exporter
    restart: unless-stopped
    command:
      - '--path.rootfs=/host'
    volumes:
      - /:/host:ro,rslave
    networks:
      - ai_internal

  dcgm-exporter:
    image: nvcr.io/nvidia/k8s/dcgm-exporter:latest
    container_name: dcgm-exporter
    restart: unless-stopped
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all
              capabilities: [gpu]
    networks:
      - ai_internal

volumes:
  prometheus_data:
  grafana_data:
  loki_data:
```

## Prometheus Configuration (monitoring/prometheus.yml)
```yaml
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
  evaluation_interval: 15s

rule_files:
  - /etc/prometheus/alerts/*.yml

alerting:
  alertmanagers:
    - static_configs:
        - targets: ['alertmanager:9093']

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'ollama'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['ollama:11434']
    metrics_path: '/metrics'
    scrape_interval: 10s

  - job_name: 'node'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['node-exporter:9100']

  - job_name: 'gpu'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['dcgm-exporter:9400']

  - job_name: 'postgres'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['postgres-exporter:9187']
```

## Alert Rules (monitoring/alerts/llm-alerts.yml)
```yaml
groups:
  - name: ollama_alerts
    interval: 30s
    rules:
      - alert: OllamaHighLatency
        expr: ollama_request_duration_seconds{quantile="0.95"} > 30
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Ollama p95 latency > 30s"
          description: "Model {{ $labels.model }} p95 latency is {{ $value }}s"

      - alert: OllamaDown
        expr: up{job="ollama"} == 0
        for: 1m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Ollama service is down"

      - alert: HighMemoryPressure
        expr: (node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes / node_memory_MemTotal_bytes) < 0.1
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Less than 10% memory available"
          description: "Available memory: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}"

      - alert: GPUHighTemperature
        expr: DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP > 85
        for: 3m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "GPU temperature critical: {{ $value }}C"

      - alert: GPUMemoryAlmostFull
        expr: (DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED / DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_TOTAL) > 0.95
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "GPU memory >95% utilized"

      - alert: PostgresDown
        expr: pg_up == 0
        for: 1m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "ruvector PostgreSQL is down"
```

## Grafana Dashboard Key Panels
Create dashboard with these panels:

1. **Request Rate**: `rate(ollama_requests_total[5m])` by model
2. **P50/P95/P99 Latency**: `histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(ollama_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))`
3. **Active Model Sessions**: `ollama_loaded_models`
4. **GPU Utilization**: `DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL`
5. **VRAM Used**: `DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED / 1024`
6. **System Memory**: `node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes / node_memory_MemTotal_bytes`
7. **Token Throughput**: `rate(ollama_tokens_total[1m])`
8. **Vector Search Latency**: Custom query from PostgreSQL

## Loki Log Queries for Common Issues
```
# OOM kills
{container="ollama"} |= "out of memory"

# Slow generations
{container="ollama"} | json | duration > 30s

# Failed requests
{container="nginx-proxy"} | pattern '<_> <status> <_>' | status >= 500

# Model load events
{container="ollama"} |= "loading model"
```

title: Backup and Disaster Recovery for Private AI Systems
content:
Comprehensive backup strategy covering ruvector knowledge base data, Ollama model storage, and configuration state. Includes RPO/RTO targets and tested recovery procedures.

## RPO/RTO Targets
| Component | RPO | RTO | Strategy |
|-----------|-----|-----|----------|
| ruvector KB (PostgreSQL) | 1 hour | 30 min | WAL streaming + daily dump |
| Ollama models | N/A (re-downloadable) | 2-4 hours | Periodic snapshot to S3/NFS |
| WebUI config/users | 24 hours | 15 min | Daily volume backup |
| Nginx/SSL config | 24 hours | 5 min | Git repository |
| Embeddings index | 1 hour | 45 min | Replicated with PG backup |

## 1. PostgreSQL Backup (ruvector KB)

### Continuous WAL Archiving
```bash
# postgresql.conf additions
archive_mode = on
archive_command = 'cp %p /backup/wal/%f'
wal_level = replica
```

### Automated Backup Script
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# /scripts/backup-ruvector.sh
set -euo pipefail

BACKUP_DIR="/backup/postgres"
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)
RETENTION_DAYS=30
DB_CONTAINER="ruvector-postgres"
DB_USER="ruvector"
DB_NAME="ruvector_kb"
S3_BUCKET="s3://company-ai-backups/ruvector"

# Create backup directory
mkdir -p $BACKUP_DIR

# Dump with compression
echo "Starting backup: $DATE"
docker exec $DB_CONTAINER pg_dump \
  -U $DB_USER \
  -d $DB_NAME \
  --format=custom \
  --compress=9 \
  --no-password \
  > $BACKUP_DIR/ruvector_${DATE}.pgdump

# Get backup size
BACKUP_SIZE=$(du -sh $BACKUP_DIR/ruvector_${DATE}.pgdump | cut -f1)
echo "Backup size: $BACKUP_SIZE"

# Verify backup integrity
docker exec $DB_CONTAINER pg_restore \
  --list /dev/stdin < $BACKUP_DIR/ruvector_${DATE}.pgdump > /dev/null
echo "Backup integrity: OK"

# Checksum
sha256sum $BACKUP_DIR/ruvector_${DATE}.pgdump > $BACKUP_DIR/ruvector_${DATE}.sha256

# Upload to S3 (optional, for off-site)
# aws s3 cp $BACKUP_DIR/ruvector_${DATE}.pgdump $S3_BUCKET/

# Prune old backups
find $BACKUP_DIR -name "*.pgdump" -mtime +${RETENTION_DAYS} -delete
find $BACKUP_DIR -name "*.sha256" -mtime +${RETENTION_DAYS} -delete

echo "Backup complete: ruvector_${DATE}.pgdump"
```

### Schedule with Cron
```bash
# /etc/cron.d/ruvector-backup
0 */2 * * * root /scripts/backup-ruvector.sh >> /var/log/ruvector-backup.log 2>&1
```

## 2. Ollama Model Backup

Ollama models are large (4-70GB) but re-downloadable. Strategy depends on connectivity:

### Internet-Connected Environments
```bash
# Just record what models are installed
docker exec ollama ollama list > /backup/ollama-models-$(date +%Y%m%d).txt

# Recovery: re-pull from list
while IFS= read -r line; do
  model=$(echo $line | awk '{print $1}')
  if [ "$model" != "NAME" ]; then
    docker exec ollama ollama pull $model
  fi
done < /backup/ollama-models-latest.txt
```

### Air-Gapped Environments
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Backup models to NAS
MODEL_SOURCE="/data/ollama/models"
BACKUP_DEST="/mnt/nas/ai-backups/ollama-models"

rsync -av \
  --progress \
  --checksum \
  --backup \
  --backup-dir="/mnt/nas/ai-backups/ollama-previous-$(date +%Y%m%d)" \
  $MODEL_SOURCE/ \
  $BACKUP_DEST/
```

## 3. Full System Recovery Runbook

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# RECOVERY PROCEDURE - run after provisioning new server

set -euo pipefail
BACKUP_DIR="/backup"
LATEST_BACKUP=$(ls -t $BACKUP_DIR/postgres/ruvector_*.pgdump | head -1)

echo "=== RECOVERY STARTING ==="
echo "Using backup: $LATEST_BACKUP"

# Step 1: Verify backup integrity
echo "[1/5] Verifying backup..."
sha256sum -c ${LATEST_BACKUP%.pgdump}.sha256

# Step 2: Start infrastructure
echo "[2/5] Starting containers..."
docker compose up -d ruvector-postgres
sleep 15

# Step 3: Restore PostgreSQL
echo "[3/5] Restoring ruvector KB..."
docker exec -i ruvector-postgres pg_restore \
  -U ruvector \
  -d ruvector_kb \
  --clean \
  --if-exists \
  < $LATEST_BACKUP

# Step 4: Restore pgvector extension
echo "[4/5] Restoring vector indexes..."
docker exec ruvector-postgres psql -U ruvector -d ruvector_kb << 'SQL'
SELECT indexname FROM pg_indexes WHERE indexdef LIKE '%hnsw%';
SELECT pg_stat_reset();
SQL

# Step 5: Start remaining services
echo "[5/5] Starting all services..."
docker compose up -d

# Verify
echo "=== VERIFICATION ==="
docker compose ps
curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/tags | python3 -m json.tool
echo "Recovery complete at $(date)"
```

## 4. Backup Monitoring
```bash
# Add to cron - alerts if backup is older than 3 hours
#!/bin/bash
LATEST=$(ls -t /backup/postgres/*.pgdump 2>/dev/null | head -1)
if [ -z "$LATEST" ]; then
  echo "CRITICAL: No backup files found"
  exit 2
fi

AGE=$(( ($(date +%s) - $(stat -c %Y $LATEST)) / 3600 ))
if [ $AGE -gt 3 ]; then
  echo "WARNING: Latest backup is ${AGE} hours old: $LATEST"
  # Send alert via webhook
  curl -s -X POST $SLACK_WEBHOOK \
    -d "{\"text\": \"ALERT: AI backup is ${AGE}h old\"}"
  exit 1
fi
echo "OK: Backup age ${AGE}h - $LATEST"
```

title: Security Hardening Runbook for Private AI Infrastructure
content:
Complete security hardening guide for private AI deployments covering network segmentation, API authentication, TLS/mTLS configuration, and secrets management.

## 1. Network Segmentation

### Docker Network Isolation
```yaml
# Separate internal from external traffic
networks:
  ai_internal:    # LLM services - never exposed externally
    driver: bridge
    internal: true  # No external routing
    ipam:
      config:
        - subnet: 172.20.0.0/24
  ai_frontend:    # Only nginx touches this
    driver: bridge
    ipam:
      config:
        - subnet: 172.21.0.0/24
```

### Host Firewall Rules (UFW)
```bash
# Only expose HTTPS externally
ufw default deny incoming
ufw default allow outgoing
ufw allow 443/tcp comment 'HTTPS only'
ufw allow 22/tcp comment 'SSH from management'

# Block direct access to internal ports
ufw deny 11434 comment 'Block Ollama direct access'
ufw deny 5435 comment 'Block PostgreSQL direct access'
ufw deny 8080 comment 'Block WebUI direct access'

ufw enable
```

## 2. API Authentication for Ollama

### Nginx API Key Authentication
```nginx
# /etc/nginx/conf.d/api-auth.conf
map $http_authorization $api_valid {
  default 0;
  "Bearer valid-token-1" 1;
  "Bearer valid-token-2" 1;
}

server {
  location /api/ {
    if ($api_valid = 0) {
      return 401 '{"error":"Unauthorized"}';
    }
    proxy_pass http://ollama:11434;
  }
}
```

### JWT Validation with nginx-lua
```bash
# Production: use proper JWT validation
# Install openresty or use nginx with lua module
apt-get install -y libnginx-mod-http-lua
```

```nginx
location /api/ {
  access_by_lua_block {
    local jwt = require "resty.jwt"
    local token = ngx.req.get_headers()["Authorization"]
    if not token then
      ngx.status = 401
      ngx.say('{"error":"Missing token"}')
      ngx.exit(401)
    end
    local verified = jwt:verify("your-jwt-secret", token:gsub("Bearer ", ""))
    if not verified["verified"] then
      ngx.status = 401
      ngx.exit(401)
    end
  }
  proxy_pass http://ollama:11434;
}
```

## 3. TLS/mTLS Between Services

### Generate Internal CA
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Generate internal CA for service-to-service mTLS
mkdir -p /etc/private-ai/certs

# CA key and certificate
openssl genrsa -out /etc/private-ai/certs/ca.key 4096
openssl req -new -x509 \
  -key /etc/private-ai/certs/ca.key \
  -out /etc/private-ai/certs/ca.crt \
  -days 3650 \
  -subj "/CN=Private-AI-Internal-CA/O=CompanyName"

# Service certificate function
generate_cert() {
  SERVICE=$1
  openssl genrsa -out /etc/private-ai/certs/${SERVICE}.key 2048
  openssl req -new \
    -key /etc/private-ai/certs/${SERVICE}.key \
    -out /etc/private-ai/certs/${SERVICE}.csr \
    -subj "/CN=${SERVICE}.ai.internal"
  openssl x509 -req \
    -in /etc/private-ai/certs/${SERVICE}.csr \
    -CA /etc/private-ai/certs/ca.crt \
    -CAkey /etc/private-ai/certs/ca.key \
    -CAcreateserial \
    -out /etc/private-ai/certs/${SERVICE}.crt \
    -days 365
}

for svc in ollama postgres webui nginx; do
  generate_cert $svc
done
```

## 4. Secrets Management

### Never Hardcode - Use Docker Secrets
```yaml
# docker-compose.yml with Docker Swarm secrets
services:
  ruvector-postgres:
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD_FILE: /run/secrets/postgres_password
    secrets:
      - postgres_password

secrets:
  postgres_password:
    external: true
```

```bash
# Create secrets (Swarm mode)
printf "$(openssl rand -base64 32)" | docker secret create postgres_password -
printf "$(openssl rand -base64 32)" | docker secret create webui_secret_key -
```

### HashiCorp Vault Integration (Enterprise)
```bash
# Pull secrets at container start
docker run --rm vault:latest \
  vault kv get -field=password secret/private-ai/postgres

# Use in entrypoint.sh
#!/bin/bash
export POSTGRES_PASSWORD=$(vault kv get -field=password secret/private-ai/postgres)
exec "$@"
```

## 5. Audit Logging
```bash
# PostgreSQL audit logging
# postgresql.conf
log_connections = on
log_disconnections = on
log_duration = on
log_min_duration_statement = 1000  # Log queries >1s
log_line_prefix = '%t [%p]: [%l-1] user=%u,db=%d,app=%a,client=%h '

# Review audit logs
docker exec ruvector-postgres psql -U ruvector -c \
  "SELECT * FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'active';"
```

## 6. Container Security
```yaml
# Run containers as non-root
services:
  open-webui:
    user: "1000:1000"
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true
    read_only: true
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp
    cap_drop:
      - ALL
    cap_add:
      - NET_BIND_SERVICE
```

## Security Checklist
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Run weekly security validation
echo "=== Security Audit ==="

# Check for exposed ports
echo "\n[Exposed Ports]"
ss -tlnp | grep -E ':(80|443|11434|5435|8080)'

# Check container users
echo "\n[Container Users]"
docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | xargs -I{} docker exec {} id 2>/dev/null

# Check for hardcoded secrets in env
echo "\n[Environment Variables - redacted]"
docker inspect ollama | jq '.[].Config.Env | map(select(test("KEY|SECRET|PASS|TOKEN"))) | map(split("=")[0])'

# Check TLS cert expiry
echo "\n[TLS Cert Expiry]"
openssl x509 -in /etc/private-ai/certs/nginx.crt -noout -enddate
```

title: The CISO Conversation: Explaining Private AI to Security Leadership
content:
A structured guide for consultants and sales engineers to navigate the skeptical CISO conversation about private AI deployment. Covers data residency versus privacy, breach liability, and the shadow AI problem.

## Opening the Conversation

The CISO has heard the pitch before. They've seen Microsoft, Google, and AWS all claim their AI is "enterprise-grade" and "private." Your opening needs to acknowledge their legitimate skepticism before presenting solutions.

**Opening line that works:**
"I'm not here to tell you cloud AI is dangerous — I'm here to show you exactly where your data goes when you use it, and then let you decide if that's acceptable for your specific regulatory environment."

## Answering: "Why Not Use the Cloud Provider's Privacy Settings?"

### The Technical Reality
When a user submits a prompt to any cloud AI endpoint — even with enterprise agreements — the following occurs:
1. The prompt traverses the public internet (even with TLS)
2. It lands on compute infrastructure you do not control
3. It is processed by a model you cannot audit
4. Logs, telemetry, and abuse-detection systems see the content
5. Your data is subject to the provider's country's laws and law enforcement access

**What "private settings" actually mean:** The provider promises not to use your data for training. They do NOT promise:
- Immunity from subpoenas or FISA court orders
- That no human reviewer ever sees your data
- That their security team cannot access logs
- Protection from provider-side data breaches

**The analogy that lands:** "Mailing a letter with 'CONFIDENTIAL' stamped on it versus sending it encrypted with keys only you hold. The postal worker still handles the envelope."

## Data Residency vs. Data Privacy: The Critical Distinction

This is the gap most CISOs initially miss.

**Data Residency** = Where data is stored geographically. EU data stored in EU data centers. This satisfies GDPR Article 44 transfer restrictions on paper.

**Data Privacy** = Whether the data is actually private. Data stored in an EU data center can still be:
- Accessed by the provider's global engineering team
- Subject to US CLOUD Act jurisdiction if the provider is US-headquartered (this overrides data residency)
- Exfiltrated if the provider suffers a breach
- Reviewed by content moderation systems

**The CISO question to ask:** "When your legal team approved the Microsoft Azure OpenAI contract, did they account for the CLOUD Act exposure?"

The US CLOUD Act (2018) allows US law enforcement to compel US-headquartered companies to produce data stored anywhere in the world. Microsoft, Google, and Amazon are all US companies. Their EU data centers do not insulate your data from US government access.

**Private AI eliminates this exposure entirely.** Data never leaves your infrastructure. There is no provider to compel. There is no foreign jurisdiction involved.

## Breach Liability Analysis

### Scenario Comparison

**Cloud AI Breach (third-party):**
- You are a victim of your vendor's breach
- You must notify affected individuals (GDPR 72-hour window, HIPAA 60-day)
- Regulators will ask: "Did you conduct adequate vendor due diligence?"
- You may still face fines for inadequate data processing agreements
- OpenAI had 100,000 account credentials exposed in 2023 alone

**Private AI Breach (on-premises):**
- You control the breach scope
- You have visibility into exactly what was accessed
- No third-party vendor to coordinate with during incident response
- Your security team knows the infrastructure

**HIPAA Math:** A breach involving PHI processed through a cloud AI tool where the BAA does not explicitly cover AI processing = possible $100,000-$1.9M fine per violation category. Many enterprise AI contracts were signed before legal teams understood this.

## The Shadow AI Problem

This is often the CISO's most immediate operational concern.

**Current state in most organizations:**
- 65-80% of employees are using personal ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini accounts for work tasks (2024 Salesforce State of IT report)
- They are pasting customer data, internal memos, source code, financial projections
- This is completely invisible to your DLP tools, CASB, or monitoring
- It violates most companies' acceptable use policies but enforcement is impossible

**The shadow AI problem is not solved by blocking.** Blocking ChatGPT.com creates workarounds (VPNs, phone hotspots). Employees do not stop; they just become invisible.

**Private AI solves shadow AI by providing a better alternative.** When employees have a fast, capable, approved AI tool that works on internal documents, they stop using personal accounts. You get:
- Visibility and logging of all AI interactions
- Auditability for compliance
- Control over what data enters the system
- A tool that actually knows your internal documents

**The CISO pitch:** "You have 500 employees using personal ChatGPT today. You cannot stop them. You can either remain blind to what they're doing with your data, or give them a better tool and get visibility back."

## Handling "We Already Evaluated This"

If the CISO says they evaluated local AI and rejected it, ask what year that was. The landscape changed dramatically:
- 2023: Local models were significantly worse than cloud options
- 2024: Llama 3, Mistral, Qwen models matched GPT-3.5 quality
- 2025: 7B-13B models match GPT-4 quality for most enterprise tasks (document Q&A, summarization, classification)

Offer a 30-day parallel pilot: run the same tasks through both systems and measure quality objectively.

title: Live Demo Script: 5-Minute Private AI Demonstration
content:
A structured, rehearsed demo flow for impressing prospects with private AI capabilities. Designed to be deliverable in 5 minutes with fallback options for technical failures.

## Pre-Demo Setup Checklist (15 minutes before)

```bash
# 1. Verify all services running
docker compose ps
# All should show: Up (healthy)

# 2. Pre-load model (avoids cold-start delay in demo)
curl http://localhost:11434/api/generate -d '{"model":"llama3.1:8b","prompt":"hello","stream":false}'

# 3. Pre-ingest demo document (use client's industry)
# For healthcare: anonymized clinical guideline PDF
# For finance: public regulatory document
# For legal: public case summary

# 4. Open network monitor
# macOS: open -a "Activity Monitor" & click Network tab
# Linux: watch -n1 'ss -s'
# Windows: Resource Monitor > Network tab

# 5. Open browser tabs pre-arranged:
#   Tab 1: Open WebUI (http://ai.demo.local)
#   Tab 2: Network monitor screenshot ready
#   Tab 3: docker stats (terminal)
```

## The 5-Minute Demo Flow

### Minute 0-1: The Setup Statement

**Say:** "Before I show you anything, I want to demonstrate something. I'm going to open our network monitoring tool right now, and I want you to watch what happens to network traffic when we use this AI."

- Open Activity Monitor / Network tab, leave it visible
- Point out current baseline traffic
- **Key setup:** "If any data left this machine to reach a cloud server, you'd see upload spikes here."

### Minute 1-2: Document Ingestion

**Say:** "I'm going to give this AI a document it has never seen. This is a [industry-specific document] I just loaded for this demo."

1. Drag a PDF into Open WebUI or use the upload button
2. Watch the ingestion progress bar
3. **Say:** "It's reading the document, breaking it into chunks, converting each chunk into a mathematical representation called an embedding, and storing those embeddings in our local vector database — all on this machine."
4. Point at the network monitor: "Notice: no network spike. Nothing left the building."

**Timing note:** Pre-ingest the document if the file is large. Switch to a pre-ingested collection for a smooth demo.

### Minute 2-4: Semantic Search and Chat

**Ask the prospect:** "What question would you ask this document in real life?"

(If they hesitate, have 3 ready for their industry.)

**Healthcare:** "What is the recommended dosing protocol for patients with renal impairment?"
**Finance:** "What are the disclosure requirements for material non-public information?"
**Legal:** "What was the court's reasoning on the negligence standard?"

1. Type the question into the chat interface
2. Watch the response stream
3. The system will cite specific sections of the document
4. **Say:** "It found the relevant section, pulled the exact text, and synthesized an answer. The citations at the bottom link directly to the source."

**Network monitor check:** "Still no outbound traffic spike. This query was answered entirely locally."

### Minute 4-5: The Proof Moment

**Say:** "Now I'm going to disconnect the network entirely."

```bash
# Disconnect network (dramatic but effective)
sudo ifconfig en0 down  # macOS
# or just pull the ethernet cable
```

**Ask:** "Is it broken?"

Type another question. It answers. Reconnect.

**Say:** "That's the difference between private AI and cloud AI. When the internet is down, or when a vendor has an outage, or when a nation-state cuts cables — your AI still works. Because it's yours."

## How to Prove No Data Egress

### Method 1: Network Monitor Visual
Activity Monitor on macOS shows per-process network usage. During an Ollama query, the Ollama process shows zero network bytes.

### Method 2: tcpdump Live Capture
```bash
# Run in terminal visible to prospect
sudo tcpdump -i any -n host 0.0.0.0/0 and not net 172.20.0.0/24
# This captures all traffic EXCEPT internal Docker network
# During a demo query: silence
```

### Method 3: Pre-prepared Screenshot Deck
If client is remote or tcpdump is too technical, show screenshots from a previous demo:
- Cloud AI query: network spike clearly visible
- Private AI query: flat line

## Fallback Scenarios

### Model is slow / loading
**Say:** "The model is loading into GPU memory — this only happens on first use. In production this would be pre-warmed." Use a smaller model (3B vs 8B) for demo machines.

### WebUI is down
```bash
# Fallback: use Ollama API directly
curl http://localhost:11434/api/generate \
  -d '{"model":"llama3.1:8b","prompt":"Summarize the main points of the document I described","stream":false}'
```
This is actually MORE impressive to technical audiences — shows the raw API.

### Wrong answer from the model
**Say:** "This is actually important to see — the system flagged that it couldn't find a direct answer in the document and is telling us rather than hallucinating. That's the retrieval system working correctly." (This works if WebUI shows "I don't have that information in the provided context.")

## Post-Demo Close

**Ask:** "What internal documents would be most valuable for your team to query like this?"

This shifts them from evaluation mode to implementation imagination. Write down their answer — it becomes your pilot project scope.

title: ROI Calculator Framework for Private AI Deployment
content:
A structured financial model for calculating the return on investment for private AI versus cloud AI, including hardware amortization, operational costs, risk avoidance, and productivity gains.

## Cost Model Structure

Total Cost of Ownership = CapEx + OpEx + Risk Cost - Productivity Gains

## Part 1: Cloud AI Cost Baseline

### Usage Estimation Template
```
Organization Size: [X employees]
Estimated AI Users: [X * 0.4 = heavy users]
Average queries/day/user: [20-100 depending on role]
Average tokens/query: [500 input + 1000 output]

Monthly token volume:
  Input:  [users] × [queries/day] × 30 × 500 = [X tokens/month]
  Output: [users] × [queries/day] × 30 × 1000 = [Y tokens/month]
```

### Cloud API Pricing (2025 rates, verify current)
| Model | Input ($/1M tokens) | Output ($/1M tokens) |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|
| GPT-4o | $2.50 | $10.00 |
| GPT-4o-mini | $0.15 | $0.60 |
| Claude Sonnet 3.5 | $3.00 | $15.00 |
| Claude Haiku | $0.25 | $1.25 |
| Gemini 1.5 Pro | $1.25 | $5.00 |

### Cloud Cost Calculation Example
```
Company: 500 employees, 200 AI users, 50 queries/day/user

Monthly tokens:
  Input:  200 × 50 × 30 × 500 = 150,000,000 tokens (150M)
  Output: 200 × 50 × 30 × 1000 = 300,000,000 tokens (300M)

Using GPT-4o:
  Input cost:  150M × $2.50/1M = $375/month
  Output cost: 300M × $10.00/1M = $3,000/month
  Monthly total: $3,375
  Annual total: $40,500

Using Claude Sonnet:
  Monthly: (150M × $3.00 + 300M × $15.00) / 1M = $450 + $4,500 = $4,950
  Annual: $59,400

Note: Enterprise tier pricing typically 20-30% lower with volume.
Note: These costs GROW with adoption — plan for 3x in year 2-3.
```

## Part 2: Private AI Infrastructure Costs

### Hardware Options by Scale

**Option A: Single Server (up to 50 concurrent users)**
```
Server with NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24GB VRAM):
  Hardware:  $8,000-12,000 (server + GPU)
  Amortized: $2,000-3,000/year (4-year lifecycle)
  
Or: NVIDIA A100 40GB (higher throughput):
  Hardware:  $15,000-25,000
  Amortized: $4,000-6,000/year
```

**Option B: Rack Server (up to 200 concurrent users)**
```
Dell/HP rack server + 2x A100 80GB:
  Hardware:  $60,000-80,000
  Amortized: $15,000-20,000/year
```

**Option C: Existing Server Repurpose**
```
Modern workstation with RTX 3090:
  Additional cost: $1,500-2,500 (GPU only)
  Amortized: $375-625/year
```

### Annual Operating Costs
```
Electricity (Option A - single GPU server):
  Power draw: ~350W average (inference load)
  Hours/year: 8,760
  kWh/year: 3,066
  Cost at $0.12/kWh: $368/year

IT Administration:
  Initial setup: 20-40 hours one-time
  Ongoing: 2-4 hours/month = 24-48 hours/year
  At $100/hour internal IT rate: $2,400-4,800/year
  (Lower with managed service provider)

Software: $0 (all open source: Ollama, Open WebUI, pgvector)

Maintenance/Updates: 1-2 hours/month = ~$1,200-2,400/year
```

### Total Annual Private AI Cost (Option A)
```
Hardware amortization:  $2,500/year
Electricity:            $370/year
IT administration:      $3,600/year
Maintenance:            $1,800/year
─────────────────────────────────────
Total Annual:           $8,270/year
```

## Part 3: Break-Even Analysis

```
Cloud AI annual cost (500 users, GPT-4o):    $40,500
Private AI annual cost:                        $8,270
Annual savings:                               $32,230

One-time setup investment:
  Hardware:          $10,000
  Implementation:    $8,000-15,000 (consultant fees)
  Total investment:  $18,000-25,000

Break-even: $22,000 ÷ $32,230/year = 8.2 months

Year 3 cumulative savings:
  Cloud: $40,500 × 3 = $121,500
  Private: $25,000 setup + $8,270 × 3 = $49,810
  Savings: $71,690
```

## Part 4: Risk Avoidance Value

### HIPAA Fine Avoidance
```
HIPAA violation categories:
  Tier 1 (unknowing): $100 - $50,000 per violation
  Tier 2 (reasonable cause): $1,000 - $50,000
  Tier 3 (willful neglect, corrected): $10,000 - $50,000
  Tier 4 (willful neglect, uncorrected): $50,000 - $1,900,000

If 50 employees each submitted PHI to cloud AI 10 times:
  = 500 violations at Tier 2 minimum
  = $500,000 minimum exposure
  = Up to $25,000,000 maximum

Risk-adjusted value (10% probability of discovery × $2M average fine):
  = $200,000 risk-adjusted annual value
```

### GDPR Fine Avoidance
```
GDPR Article 83(4): Up to €10M or 2% global annual turnover
GDPR Article 83(5): Up to €20M or 4% global annual turnover

For a €100M revenue company:
  Maximum exposure: €4M per incident
  Risk-adjusted (5% probability): €200,000/year
```

### Shadow AI Elimination Value
```
Shadow AI incidents (data exfiltration via employee personal accounts):
  Industry average cost of data breach (IBM 2024): $4.88M
  Percentage involving insider/employee action: 35%
  Shadow AI contribution: estimated 10-15%
  
  Risk-adjusted value of eliminating shadow AI:
  $4.88M × 35% × 12% = $204,960/year risk reduction
```

## Part 5: Productivity Gains

```
Knowledge worker productivity with AI assistance:
  McKinsey (2024): 30-50% improvement on knowledge tasks
  Conservative estimate for enterprise adoption: 15%

200 AI users × $80,000 average salary × 15% efficiency gain:

Note: Not all of this is captured as cost savings — much is capacity
expansion. Use 10% as conservative captured value:
  = $240,000/year
```

title: Objection Handling Guide for Private AI Sales
content:
Proven responses to the most common objections encountered when selling private AI deployments to enterprise clients. Includes data-backed responses, trial close techniques, and concession strategies.

## Objection 1: "Local AI Is Slower"

### The Full Response

**Acknowledge first:** "You're right that raw token generation speed on consumer hardware is lower than a hyperscaler with 10,000 GPUs. Let's talk about what speed actually matters for your use case."

**Reframe the question:**
- Cloud AI response: 50-200ms network latency PLUS model processing time
- Local AI response: 0ms network latency PLUS model processing time
- For short queries on a modern GPU: local can actually be faster end-to-end

**The benchmark response:**
```
Task: Summarize a 10-page document
  Cloud GPT-4o:        8-15 seconds (includes network)
  Local Llama 3.1 8B:  12-20 seconds (consumer GPU)
  Local Llama 3.1 8B:  4-8 seconds (professional GPU, A100)

Task: Answer a specific question from a document
  Cloud GPT-4o:        3-6 seconds
  Local 8B model:      5-10 seconds (consumer GPU)
  Local 8B model:      2-4 seconds (A100 GPU)

Task: Classify 1,000 documents (batch)
  Cloud: $15-50 in API costs + rate limiting delays
  Local: $0 + no rate limits, runs overnight unattended
```

**The trial close:** "Would you be willing to run a 30-day parallel pilot? We'll run the same tasks through both systems and measure actual time-to-value, not just token speed. I'm confident the productivity impact will be equivalent or better."

## Objection 2: "Local Models Aren't as Smart"

### The Full Response

**Acknowledge the historical reality:** "A year ago, this was unquestionably true. Llama 2 was noticeably weaker than GPT-4. The gap has closed dramatically."

**Current benchmark data (2025):**
```
MMBench (general knowledge): 
  GPT-4o: 83.4
  Llama 3.1 70B: 82.1  ← within 2%

HumanEval (coding):
  GPT-4: 87%
  Llama 3.1 8B: 73%  ← meaningful gap for complex code
  Llama 3.1 70B: 84%  ← near parity

Document Q&A (RAG tasks):
  GPT-4o: 91% accuracy
  Llama 3.1 8B + RAG: 88% accuracy  ← 3% gap
  Mistral 7B + RAG: 86% accuracy
```

**Task-appropriate model selection:**
"The key insight is that enterprise AI is mostly NOT about reasoning about complex novel problems. The top use cases are:
1. Document Q&A: 7-8B models are 90%+ as good as GPT-4
2. Summarization: 7-8B models match GPT-4 quality
3. Classification: 3-7B models often exceed GPT-4 (fine-tunable)
4. Meeting transcription/summary: 7B models are sufficient
5. Code completion (simple): 7B models work well

The use cases where cloud models are still clearly better:
1. Complex multi-step reasoning
2. Creative writing requiring nuance
3. Very recent knowledge (post training cutoff)"

**The proposition:** "For 85% of enterprise use cases, a well-configured local 8-13B model delivers equivalent business value. We fine-tune and RAG-enable it to know your specific domain, which often makes it MORE accurate than a generic cloud model."

## Objection 3: "It's Too Complex to Maintain"

### The Full Response

**Acknowledge:** "It is more complex than clicking 'Enable AI' in Microsoft 365. Let's quantify what that complexity actually means."

**Maintenance reality:**
```
Weekly maintenance (steady state):
  Review monitoring dashboard:           10 minutes
  Check backup completion:               5 minutes
  
Monthly maintenance:
  Update containers (automated):         30 minutes
  Review user access and logs:           20 minutes
  Test restore from backup:              30 minutes
  
Quarterly:
  Review and update models:              2 hours
  Security patch review:                 1 hour
  Performance review:                    1 hour
  
Total annual IT time: ~100-120 hours
At $100/hour internal rate: $10,000-12,000/year equivalent
```

**Managed deployment options:**
"We offer three tiers:
1. **Self-managed** (you own everything): Lowest cost, requires internal IT skill
2. **Managed updates** (we handle patches, updates, model upgrades): $2,000-4,000/month
3. **Full managed service** (we monitor, maintain, on-call support): $5,000-8,000/month"

**The comparison:** "Microsoft 365 Copilot costs $30/user/month. For 500 users that's $180,000/year — and you still need IT staff to administer Azure AD, Intune, and the M365 environment. The total IT burden comparison is not as lopsided as it first appears."

## Objection 4: "We Already Have Microsoft Copilot"

### The Full Response

**Affirm the investment:** "Microsoft 365 Copilot is a good product for certain tasks. Let's be precise about what it does and doesn't do."

**What Copilot does well:**
- Drafting emails in Outlook context
- Summarizing Teams meetings
- Working within Office documents you already have

**What Copilot doesn't do:**
- Your data still leaves Microsoft's control and is subject to their terms
- No support for your custom internal systems and databases
- Cannot query your proprietary databases or internal wikis
- Requires Microsoft 365 E3/E5 license base ($57-67/user/month before Copilot add-on)
- The Copilot add-on is $30/user/month additional
- Rate limits and availability tied to Microsoft's infrastructure

**The data question:** "With Copilot, your employees' queries and the documents they reference are processed by Microsoft. For public documents this is fine. For M&A analysis, litigation strategy, employee performance data, competitive intelligence — does your legal team know this data pattern?"

**The augmentation framing:** "Private AI doesn't replace Copilot for Office tasks. It gives you a second, unrestricted AI for sensitive workflows, custom applications, and data that cannot leave your control. They serve different roles."

## Objection 5: "The Budget Isn't There Right Now"

### The Full Response

**Reframe as cost avoidance:** "I understand budget constraints. Let me show you this differently — what is your current annual spend on cloud AI tools across all departments, including approved and shadow usage?"

**Phased implementation:**
```
Phase 1 (Month 1-2): $3,000-5,000
  Single server with consumer GPU
  Pilot with 20-30 users
  Prove ROI before scaling
  
Phase 2 (Month 3-6): $10,000-15,000 if pilot succeeds
  Professional GPU hardware
  100-200 users
  Custom model fine-tuning
  
Phase 3 (Year 2+): $0 additional CapEx
  Scale users on existing infrastructure
  Ongoing managed service option
```

**ROI payback framing:** "The Phase 1 pilot costs $5,000 and takes 6 weeks to deploy. If it replaces $3,000/month in cloud AI subscriptions, it pays for itself in under 2 months. Would you like to start with a 20-user proof of concept?"
