title: Ollama Model Selection Guide for Enterprise Use Cases
content:
A practical guide for selecting the right local LLM model for specific enterprise tasks, covering model families, hardware requirements, and task-fit analysis.

## Model Selection Decision Framework

Model selection depends on four factors: task type, hardware available, latency requirements, and accuracy needs. Do not default to the largest model — bigger is not always better for RAG-heavy enterprise workflows.

## Model Families and Characteristics

### Llama 3.x Series (Meta)
- **Llama 3.2 3B**: Fastest, lowest VRAM (2GB), good for classification and short summaries
- **Llama 3.1 8B**: Balanced speed/quality, 8GB VRAM, best for document Q&A
- **Llama 3.1 70B**: Near GPT-4 quality, 48GB VRAM (2x A100 or H100), complex reasoning
- **License**: Llama Community License (commercial use allowed with attribution)

### Mistral / Mixtral Series
- **Mistral 7B**: Punches above weight, 8GB VRAM, strong instruction following
- **Mixtral 8x7B**: Mixture-of-Experts, ~26GB VRAM, excellent for multi-domain knowledge
- **Mistral Nemo 12B**: Excellent multilingual, 12GB VRAM
- **License**: Apache 2.0 (fully open, no restrictions)

### Qwen 2.5 Series (Alibaba)
- **Qwen2.5 7B**: Best-in-class at 7B size, strong math and code
- **Qwen2.5 72B**: Competitive with GPT-4 on benchmarks, 48GB VRAM
- **License**: Apache 2.0 (most sizes), Qwen License (72B)

### Phi Series (Microsoft)
- **Phi-3 Mini 3.8B**: Remarkable quality at tiny size, 4GB VRAM
- **Phi-3 Medium 14B**: Best efficiency at medium size, 14GB VRAM
- **License**: MIT (fully open)

### Embedding Models (Required for RAG)
- **nomic-embed-text**: Default choice, 768 dimensions, excellent retrieval
- **mxbai-embed-large**: Higher accuracy, 1024 dimensions, 1.5x slower
- **all-minilm**: Fast and small, good for high-volume indexing

## Task-to-Model Matrix

| Use Case | Recommended Model | VRAM | Accuracy | Speed |
|----------|-----------------|------|----------|-------|
| Document Q&A (RAG) | Llama 3.1 8B | 8GB | High | Fast |
| Email/document drafting | Mistral 7B | 8GB | High | Fast |
| Code review/generation | Qwen2.5 7B | 8GB | High | Fast |
| Complex analysis | Llama 3.1 70B | 48GB | Very High | Slow |
| Classification (bulk) | Llama 3.2 3B | 2GB | Medium | Very Fast |
| Multilingual | Mistral Nemo 12B | 12GB | High | Medium |
| Math/financial | Qwen2.5 14B | 14GB | High | Medium |
| Quick extraction | Phi-3 Mini | 4GB | Medium | Very Fast |

## Hardware-to-Model Matching

```bash
# Check what's available
nvidia-smi --query-gpu=name,memory.total --format=csv

# 8GB VRAM (RTX 3070, RTX 4070)
ollama pull llama3.1:8b          # Primary model
ollama pull nomic-embed-text      # Embeddings

# 16GB VRAM (RTX 3080, RTX 4080)
ollama pull mistral:7b
ollama pull llama3.1:8b
ollama pull phi3:medium           # Can run two models concurrently
ollama pull nomic-embed-text

# 24GB VRAM (RTX 3090, RTX 4090)
ollama pull llama3.1:8b
ollama pull mixtral:8x7b          # ~26GB - needs quantization
ollama pull llama3.1:8b-q8_0     # 8-bit quantization fits in 24GB

# 40GB VRAM (A100 40GB)
ollama pull llama3.1:8b
ollama pull llama3.1:70b-q4_0    # 4-bit quantization of 70B

# 80GB+ VRAM (A100 80GB, H100)
ollama pull llama3.1:70b          # Full precision 70B
ollama pull qwen2.5:72b
```

## Quantization Guide

Quantization reduces model size and VRAM requirements at modest accuracy cost:

```
llama3.1:8b          = Full precision (~16GB)
llama3.1:8b-q8_0     = 8-bit quantized (~9GB, <1% accuracy loss)
llama3.1:8b-q5_K_M   = 5-bit quantized (~6GB, ~2% accuracy loss)
llama3.1:8b-q4_K_M   = 4-bit quantized (~5GB, ~3-5% accuracy loss)
llama3.1:8b-q3_K_S   = 3-bit quantized (~4GB, >5% accuracy loss)

Recommendation: q4_K_M is the sweet spot for most deployments
```

## Modelfile Customization for Enterprise

```dockerfile
# /etc/ollama/Modelfiles/enterprise-assistant
FROM llama3.1:8b

SYSTEM """
You are an AI assistant for [Company Name]. You help employees with:
- Analyzing internal documents and policies
- Answering questions about company procedures  
- Drafting professional communications

IMPORTANT RULES:
1. Only answer based on information provided in documents or your training
2. If you are not certain, say so explicitly
3. Never make up specific numbers, dates, or names
4. For legal or medical questions, always recommend consulting qualified professionals
5. Do not discuss competitors or make comparative claims
"""

PARAMETER temperature 0.3
PARAMETER top_p 0.9
PARAMETER num_ctx 8192
PARAMETER repeat_penalty 1.1
```

```bash
# Create the custom model
ollama create company-assistant -f /etc/ollama/Modelfiles/enterprise-assistant
ollama run company-assistant
```

## Model Update Strategy

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Quarterly model update script
# Run in off-hours to avoid service disruption

MODELS=("llama3.1:8b" "nomic-embed-text" "mistral:7b")

for model in "${MODELS[@]}"; do
  echo "Checking for updates: $model"
  
  # Pull latest version
  ollama pull $model
  
  # Verify model loaded correctly
  response=$(curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/generate \
    -d "{\"model\":\"$model\",\"prompt\":\"respond with ok\",\"stream\":false}")
  
  if echo $response | grep -q "ok\|OK"; then
    echo "Updated successfully: $model"
  else
    echo "ERROR: Model verification failed for $model"
    # Rollback would require keeping previous version tag
  fi
done
```

title: ruvector PostgreSQL Schema and Vector Search Patterns for Private AI
content:
Reference guide for the ruvector knowledge base schema, HNSW index configuration, and optimized query patterns for private AI deployments.

## Schema Setup

```sql
-- Initialize extensions
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm;
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS unaccent;

-- Main knowledge base table
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS knowledge_base (
  id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  title       TEXT NOT NULL,
  content     TEXT NOT NULL,
  source      TEXT,
  source_type TEXT DEFAULT 'document',  -- document|web|manual|api
  category    TEXT,
  tags        TEXT[] DEFAULT '{}',
  metadata    JSONB DEFAULT '{}',
  embedding   VECTOR(768),  -- nomic-embed-text dimensions
  content_hash TEXT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (md5(content)) STORED,
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  indexed_at  TIMESTAMPTZ
);

-- Conversation history for context
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS conversations (
  id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  session_id  TEXT NOT NULL,
  user_id     TEXT,
  role        TEXT CHECK (role IN ('user', 'assistant', 'system')),
  content     TEXT NOT NULL,
  model       TEXT,
  tokens_used INTEGER,
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Document chunks for large file RAG
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS document_chunks (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  document_id   UUID REFERENCES knowledge_base(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  chunk_index   INTEGER NOT NULL,
  chunk_content TEXT NOT NULL,
  embedding     VECTOR(768),
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Audit log for compliance
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ai_audit_log (
  id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id     TEXT,
  action      TEXT NOT NULL,
  query       TEXT,
  model       TEXT,
  documents_referenced UUID[],
  tokens_used INTEGER,
  latency_ms  INTEGER,
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

## HNSW Index Configuration

```sql
-- HNSW index for knowledge base (primary search)
-- m=16: number of connections per layer (higher = better recall, more memory)
-- ef_construction=128: build-time accuracy (higher = better quality index)
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_kb_embedding_hnsw
  ON knowledge_base USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops)
  WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 128);

-- HNSW index for document chunks
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_chunks_embedding_hnsw
  ON document_chunks USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops)
  WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 128);

-- Full text search index (hybrid search)
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_kb_content_fts
  ON knowledge_base USING gin(to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content));

-- Tags index for filtered search
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_kb_tags
  ON knowledge_base USING gin(tags);

-- Category index for filtered search
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_kb_category
  ON knowledge_base(category);

-- Set search accuracy for queries (higher = more accurate, slower)
SET hnsw.ef_search = 100;
```

## Core Search Queries

### Pure Vector Search
```sql
-- Basic semantic similarity search
SELECT
  id,
  title,
  content,
  category,
  tags,
  1 - (embedding <=> $1::vector) AS similarity
FROM knowledge_base
WHERE embedding IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::vector
LIMIT $2;
-- $1 = query embedding (768-dim vector)
-- $2 = result count (typically 5-10)
```

### Hybrid Search (Vector + Full Text)
```sql
-- Hybrid search combining semantic and keyword relevance
-- RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion) combines both rankings
WITH vector_search AS (
  SELECT id, title, content, category,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::vector) AS vector_rank
  FROM knowledge_base
  WHERE embedding IS NOT NULL
  ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::vector
  LIMIT 50
),
keyword_search AS (
  SELECT id, title, content, category,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY ts_rank_cd(
      to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content),
      plainto_tsquery('english', $2)
    ) DESC) AS keyword_rank
  FROM knowledge_base
  WHERE to_tsvector('english', title || ' ' || content) 
    @@ plainto_tsquery('english', $2)
  LIMIT 50
)
SELECT
  COALESCE(v.id, k.id) AS id,
  COALESCE(v.title, k.title) AS title,
  COALESCE(v.content, k.content) AS content,
  (COALESCE(1.0 / (60 + v.vector_rank), 0) +
   COALESCE(1.0 / (60 + k.keyword_rank), 0)) AS rrf_score
FROM vector_search v
FULL OUTER JOIN keyword_search k ON v.id = k.id
ORDER BY rrf_score DESC
LIMIT $3;
-- $1 = query embedding, $2 = query text, $3 = result count
```

### Filtered Vector Search
```sql
-- Search within a specific category and tags
SELECT
  id, title, content,
  1 - (embedding <=> $1::vector) AS similarity
FROM knowledge_base
WHERE
  embedding IS NOT NULL
  AND category = $2
  AND tags @> $3  -- contains all specified tags
  AND 1 - (embedding <=> $1::vector) > 0.7  -- minimum similarity threshold
ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::vector
LIMIT 10;
```

## Ingestion Pattern

```javascript
// Node.js ingestion example
const { Pool } = require('pg');

const pool = new Pool({
  host: 'localhost',
  port: 5435,
  database: 'ruvector_kb',
  user: process.env.DB_USER,
  // Auth via ~/.pgpass - never inline passwords
});

async function ingestDocument(title, content, category, tags) {
  // 1. Generate embedding via Ollama
  const embedResponse = await fetch('http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      model: 'nomic-embed-text',
      prompt: `${title}\n\n${content}`
    })
  });
  const { embedding } = await embedResponse.json();

  // 2. Upsert to ruvector (avoid duplicates via content hash)
  const result = await pool.query(`
    INSERT INTO knowledge_base (title, content, category, tags, embedding)
    VALUES ($1, $2, $3, $4, $5::vector)
    ON CONFLICT (content_hash) DO UPDATE SET
      updated_at = NOW(),
      embedding = EXCLUDED.embedding
    RETURNING id
  `, [title, content, category, tags, JSON.stringify(embedding)]);

  return result.rows[0].id;
}
```

## Performance Tuning

```sql
-- PostgreSQL configuration for vector workloads
-- Add to postgresql.conf

-- Memory: allow more RAM for vector operations
shared_buffers = 4GB              -- 25% of system RAM
work_mem = 256MB                  -- Per-query sort/hash memory
maintenance_work_mem = 1GB        -- For index builds

-- Parallel query for large datasets
max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 4
max_parallel_workers = 8

-- HNSW-specific
-- Higher ef_search = better recall at cost of speed
-- Test with your data: typically 64-200 is the useful range
SET hnsw.ef_search = 100;

-- Monitor index usage
SELECT schemaname, tablename, indexname, idx_scan, idx_tup_read
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE indexname LIKE '%hnsw%'
ORDER BY idx_scan DESC;
```

title: Private AI User Onboarding and Training Guide
content:
A structured onboarding program for enterprise employees being introduced to the private AI system. Covers what the system can and cannot do, best practices for prompting, and department-specific use cases.

## Training Program Overview

**Duration:** 60 minutes total (30 min group session + 30 min self-paced)
**Audience:** All staff users
**Prerequisites:** None
**Outcome:** User can confidently use the system for their primary use case

## Section 1: What This System Is (and Isn't)

### What It Is
This is a private AI assistant that:
- Reads and understands your documents
- Answers questions based on what it finds
- Stays completely within our network - nothing leaves the building
- Learns from the documents we give it (not from internet browsing)

### What It Is Not
- It is NOT connected to the internet
- It does NOT have today's news or recent information
- It is NOT infallible - always verify important facts
- It does NOT remember your previous conversations (each session starts fresh)
- It CANNOT take actions like sending emails or updating databases

### The Privacy Guarantee
"When you type something into this system, it is processed on a server in [location] that we fully control. No query, no document, and no response ever travels to Microsoft, Google, OpenAI, or any other external service."

**Demonstration:** Show the network monitor during a query. Zero external traffic.

## Section 2: How to Ask Good Questions

### The Difference Between Good and Bad Prompts

**Poor prompt:**
```
"Tell me about leave policy"
```
**Why it fails:** Too vague. The system doesn't know what aspect, for whom, in what scenario.

**Good prompt:**
```
"Based on the HR policy documents, what is the process for requesting extended medical leave, and what documentation is required from the employee?"
```
**Why it works:** Specific, references the source, asks for both the process and a specific detail.

### The AREA Framework for Prompting
- **A**ction: What do you want the AI to DO? (summarize, find, draft, compare, explain)
- **R**eference: Which documents or context should it use?
- **E**xpected format: How should the answer look? (bullet points, table, paragraph, step-by-step)
- **A**udience: Who is this for? (client, internal team, executive, technical)

**Example applying AREA:**
```
[Action] Summarize the key differences between
[Reference] the 2023 and 2024 compliance requirements in the attached regulatory documents
[Expected format] as a comparison table
[Audience] for our compliance team to review
```

## Section 3: Department-Specific Use Cases

### Legal / Compliance
```
Good use cases:
- "Find all references to indemnification clauses across these contracts"
- "Compare the liability language in these two vendor agreements"
- "Summarize the regulatory requirements in this filing for a non-lawyer audience"
- "What are the key dates and obligations in this contract?"

Not ideal:
- "Is this contract legal?" (Requires legal judgment, not just information retrieval)
- "What happened in court case X last week?" (No internet access)
```

### HR / People Operations
```
Good use cases:
- "Draft a job description for a [role] based on our standard template"
- "What does our policy say about remote work for employees in different states?"
- "Summarize the key points from these five candidate assessments"
- "What benefits questions does our handbook answer about parental leave?"

Not ideal:
- "Should we hire this person?" (Hiring decisions require human judgment)
```

### Finance / Accounting
```
Good use cases:
- "Extract all line items over $10,000 from these invoices"
- "Summarize the Q3 variances from this report"
- "What budget categories have remaining allocation per the attached spreadsheet?"
- "Draft a memo explaining the revenue recognition methodology in this document"

Not ideal:
- "What is the stock price of X?" (No internet/live data)
- "Is this a good investment?" (Requires professional judgment)
```

### Operations / IT
```
Good use cases:
- "Find all procedures related to incident escalation in the runbook"
- "Summarize the configuration requirements from this vendor documentation"
- "Draft a change request based on this technical specification"
- "What are the troubleshooting steps for this error type per the knowledge base?"
```

## Section 4: What to Do When It Gets Something Wrong

### Recognizing Hallucinations
The AI may occasionally state something confidently that is incorrect. Signs to watch for:
- Specific statistics or numbers without a clear source citation
- Names or dates that seem oddly specific
- Contradicts something you know to be true
- The cited document section does not actually say what the AI claims

### What To Do
1. Ask it to cite the exact passage: "Quote the exact text from the document that supports this answer"
2. If it cannot quote it, treat the answer as unreliable
3. Report persistent errors to [IT contact] - some may indicate a document indexing issue
4. Never use AI output without verification for anything consequential (legal, financial, medical)

## Section 5: Data Handling Guidelines

### What Is Appropriate to Enter
- Internal policy documents
- Vendor contracts (after any necessary confidentiality review)
- Internal reports and analyses
- Meeting notes and summaries
- Technical documentation
- Your own work product

### What Requires Caution
- Personal data about employees (check with HR/Legal before ingesting)
- Customer PII (check data processing agreements)
- Attorney-client privileged communications (check with Legal)
- Information under NDA from third parties

### Logging and Audit
"All queries to the system are logged for security and compliance purposes. Logs include the query text, the documents referenced, and the model used. Logs are reviewed by [role] and retained for [period] per our data retention policy."

title: Network Architecture Patterns for Private AI Deployment
content:
Reference patterns for integrating private AI infrastructure into existing enterprise network architectures, covering DMZ placement, VLAN segmentation, VPN access, and zero-trust patterns.

## Pattern 1: Single-Site On-Premises Deployment

```
INTERNET
    │
    ▼
[Firewall/UTM]
    │
    ├─── [DMZ VLAN 10] ──────────────────────────────────
    │         No AI services here (no internet exposure)
    │
    └─── [Corporate VLAN 20] ─────────────────────────────
              │
              ├─── [User Workstations]
              ├─── [Existing Servers]
              └─── [AI Subnet VLAN 30] ──────────────────
                        │
                        ├─── AI-Server-01 (Ollama + ruvector)
                        │      10.30.0.10
                        ├─── Nginx Proxy
                        │      10.30.0.5
                        └─── Monitoring Stack
                               10.30.0.20

Firewall Rules:
  VLAN 20 → VLAN 30 (port 443): ALLOW (users to AI)
  VLAN 30 → Internet: BLOCK (AI never reaches out)
  VLAN 30 → VLAN 20 (specific ports): ALLOW (logging)
  Any → VLAN 30 (port 11434): BLOCK (Ollama never direct)
```

## Pattern 2: Multi-Site with Centralized AI

```
[HEADQUARTERS]
  AI Infrastructure
  10.0.30.0/24
       │
       │ (Site-to-Site VPN / MPLS)
       │
  ┌────┴────┐
  │         │
[BRANCH A]  [BRANCH B]
10.1.0.0/24  10.2.0.0/24

Branch users access HQ AI over:
  Option A: Existing MPLS/SD-WAN (preferred - no latency)
  Option B: IPSec VPN tunnel
  Option C: Split-tunnel VPN (only AI traffic tunneled)

Latency targets:
  Same-site: <5ms (LAN)
  MPLS branch: <20ms (acceptable)
  Internet VPN: <50ms (acceptable but watch for user complaints)
  Internet VPN >100ms: Deploy local AI node instead
```

## Pattern 3: Zero-Trust Integration

```bash
# Zero-trust: every user authenticated before AI access
# Even internal network users must authenticate

# Integration with existing identity provider:
# Option A: LDAP/Active Directory
nginx config:
  auth_request /auth;
  auth_request_set $auth_status $upstream_status;

# Option B: SAML SSO (Okta, Azure AD)
# Open WebUI supports SAML natively
WEBUI_AUTH=true
OAUTH_CLIENT_ID=your-app-client-id
OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET=your-app-client-secret
OPENID_PROVIDER_URL=https://your-org.okta.com/.well-known/openid-configuration

# Option C: Certificate-based auth (mTLS)
# Users present client certificate to access AI
nginx config:
  ssl_verify_client on;
  ssl_client_certificate /etc/nginx/ssl/ca.crt;
```

## Pattern 4: Air-Gapped Production Pattern

```
[CLASSIFIED / AIR-GAPPED NETWORK]
      No connections to corporate or internet
      Physical separation
      ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
      │                                 │
      │  [Air-Gapped AI Server]         │
      │    Ollama (local models only)   │
      │    ruvector PostgreSQL          │
      │    Open WebUI                   │
      │                                 │
      │  [Internal Registry]            │
      │    Docker images cached         │
      │    Models pre-loaded            │
      │                                 │
      │  [Workstations]                 │
      │    Browser-only access          │
      │                                 │
      └─────────────────────────────────┘
             Data diode if needed →
             (one-way transfer only)
```

## DNS Configuration

```bash
# Internal DNS records (bind9 or Windows DNS)
# Users access ai.internal.company.com

# /etc/bind/zones/company.internal.zone
$ORIGIN company.internal.
$TTL 300
ai    A    10.30.0.5    ; Nginx proxy IP

# Split DNS: internal resolves to internal IP
# External DNS: no record (not reachable)

# Test internal resolution:
nslookup ai.internal.company.com 10.0.0.1
# Should return 10.30.0.5
```

## Load Balancing for High Availability

```nginx
# Multiple Ollama instances behind nginx
upstream ollama_cluster {
  least_conn;  # Route to least loaded server
  server 10.30.0.11:11434 weight=1;
  server 10.30.0.12:11434 weight=1;
  server 10.30.0.13:11434 backup;  # Only used if others fail
  keepalive 32;
}

server {
  location /api/ {
    proxy_pass http://ollama_cluster;
    proxy_connect_timeout 10s;
    proxy_read_timeout 300s;  # Long timeout for large generations
    proxy_next_upstream error timeout;  # Failover on error
  }
}
```

## Network Monitoring for AI Traffic

```bash
# Monitor AI service network traffic
# No external traffic should appear from AI subnet

# Real-time monitoring
watch -n5 'ss -tn src 10.30.0.0/24 | grep ESTAB | grep -v 10.30.0'
# If this shows non-10.30.x destinations: ALERT

# Packet capture for verification
tcpdump -i eth0 \
  -n \
  src net 10.30.0.0/24 \
  and not dst net 10.30.0.0/24 \
  and not dst net 10.0.0.0/8
# Should be silent in a properly isolated deployment

# Prometheus alert for unexpected outbound
- alert: AIServerExternalConnection
  expr: sum(rate(node_network_transmit_bytes_total{device="eth0",instance=~"10.30.*"}[5m])) > 1000000
  annotations:
    summary: "AI server sending unexpected external traffic"
```

title: Private AI Competitive Positioning: vs Microsoft Copilot, Google Vertex, AWS Bedrock
content:
Detailed competitive analysis for positioning private AI against major cloud AI offerings in enterprise sales situations. Includes factual comparisons, messaging frameworks, and win/loss analysis.

## The Competitive Landscape

Enterprise buyers typically evaluate private AI against four incumbent options:
1. **Microsoft 365 Copilot** - most common existing deployment
2. **Google Vertex AI / Gemini for Workspace** - prevalent in Google-shop organizations
3. **AWS Bedrock** - developer-led adoption in AWS-heavy environments
4. **Azure OpenAI Service** - direct GPT-4 access with enterprise terms

## Head-to-Head: Private AI vs Microsoft 365 Copilot

| Dimension | Microsoft Copilot | Private AI |
|-----------|------------------|------------|
| Cost per user/month | $30 + M365 E3/E5 base | $15-40 (amortized) |
| Data stays on-premises | No (Microsoft cloud) | Yes (absolute) |
| Custom knowledge base | SharePoint only | Any document, any system |
| Models available | GPT-4 (Microsoft controlled) | Any open model |
| Regulatory compliance | Complex - data in Azure | Simple - your infrastructure |
| Integration | Microsoft ecosystem only | Any API, any system |
| Rate limits | Yes (per tenant) | None |
| Offline capability | No | Yes |
| Audit log ownership | Microsoft-held | You own it |

**Key Microsoft Copilot vulnerability:** Organizations using Copilot still have their data processed on Microsoft's infrastructure. The Microsoft CLOUD Act exposure still applies. For highly regulated industries (healthcare, defense, legal), this is a meaningful risk that Copilot's enterprise agreement does not fully mitigate.

**Where Microsoft wins:** Organizations fully standardized on M365 with low sensitivity data, where Copilot's tight Office integration adds immediate value.

**Your positioning against Microsoft:** "Copilot is excellent for drafting emails and summarizing Teams meetings. Private AI is for everything else — your sensitive documents, your internal databases, your workflows that cannot touch Microsoft's servers. They're not competing products; you need both."

## Head-to-Head: Private AI vs Google Vertex AI

| Dimension | Google Vertex | Private AI |
|-----------|--------------|------------|
| Infrastructure | Google Cloud | Your hardware |
| GDPR compliance | Complex | Complete |
| Cost model | Pay per token | Fixed (hardware) |
| Technical complexity | High (MLOps required) | Medium |
| Model selection | Gemini models | Any open model |
| Latency | Variable (network) | Predictable (LAN) |

**Google Vertex vulnerability:** Requires significant MLOps expertise to deploy and manage. For most enterprises, this means ongoing dependency on Google professional services or expensive cloud ML engineers. The total cost of ownership is higher than the token pricing suggests.

## Head-to-Head: Private AI vs AWS Bedrock

| Dimension | AWS Bedrock | Private AI |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| Model variety | High (many providers) | Very high (any open model) |
| Infrastructure | AWS | Your hardware |
| Fine-tuning | Available (expensive) | Available (free) |
| Data sovereignty | AWS region | Your premises |
| Network dependency | Always-on required | None |
| Cost predictability | Variable | Predictable |

**AWS Bedrock vulnerability:** Even with AWS's enterprise agreements, data processed through Bedrock is subject to AWS's privacy policy and legal jurisdiction. Organizations in GDPR-regulated environments must carefully assess whether Bedrock's data processing agreements meet Article 28 requirements.

## Objection: "But They Have Enterprise Agreements That Cover This"

This is the most sophisticated objection and requires the most nuanced response.

**The honest answer:** Enterprise agreements with Microsoft, Google, and AWS do provide meaningful privacy protections — they are contractual commitments not to use your data for training, to implement technical safeguards, and to allow audit rights.

**What enterprise agreements don't cover:**
1. **Government access:** The CLOUD Act (US), similar UK legislation, and various national security frameworks allow governments to compel cloud providers to produce data without notifying the data owner. Enterprise agreements cannot contractually override law.
2. **Breach at the provider:** Even with a contract saying "we won't expose your data," a breach at a cloud provider exposes your data. You become a victim of their security failure.
3. **Subprocessors:** Cloud AI providers use subprocessors (content moderation vendors, GPU compute providers) who have their own data access policies.
4. **Future policy changes:** An enterprise agreement covers current commitments. It cannot guarantee the provider's privacy approach in three years.

**The private AI proposition:** "Private AI provides privacy by architecture rather than privacy by contract. No agreement to trust, no auditor to believe, no jurisdiction to navigate. The data physically cannot leave your infrastructure."

## Win/Loss Analysis Patterns

### Deals Private AI Wins
- Healthcare organizations with PHI processing requirements
- Legal firms handling privileged communications
- Defense contractors with ITAR/CUI requirements
- Financial institutions with regulatory data restrictions
- Organizations with recent security breaches (heightened awareness)
- Multi-national orgs with EU data residency requirements + US parent
- Organizations with poor internet connectivity (manufacturing, remote sites)

### Deals Private AI Loses
- Organizations deeply invested in Microsoft 365 with limited budget
- Teams wanting the absolute latest frontier model capabilities
- Startups without IT infrastructure to maintain servers
- Organizations where the primary use case is internet-search-augmented AI

### Pricing Strategy
**Never lead with price.** Lead with compliance requirements and risk reduction. Price becomes secondary when the alternative is a regulatory fine or a breach headline.

If forced to compete on price:
- Calculate the 3-year TCO including cloud subscription growth
- Include risk-adjusted cost of breach or non-compliance
- Present the break-even analysis (typically 8-18 months)
- Offer a phased pilot to reduce initial commitment

## Messaging Framework by Buyer Type

| Buyer | Primary Concern | Your Message |
|-------|----------------|-------------|
| CISO | Data exposure, breach liability | Architecture-level privacy, no vendor trust required |
| CFO | Cost predictability, ROI | Fixed costs vs variable cloud, 3-year TCO |
| CTO | Technical capabilities, maintenance | Full control, any model, no vendor lock-in |
| Compliance | Regulatory adherence | Complete audit trail, on-premises processing |
| End user | Quality, speed, ease of use | Task-appropriate models, no rate limits, internal docs |

title: Private AI Performance Benchmarking for Client Validation
content:
A structured benchmarking framework for validating private AI performance against client expectations and cloud alternatives. Includes test scripts, metrics collection, and reporting templates.

## Why Benchmarking Matters in Sales

Clients evaluating private AI have two fears: speed and quality. Benchmarking addresses both with objective data instead of promises. Run these benchmarks during the pilot phase and use the results to prove business case.

## Benchmark Suite 1: Throughput Testing

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# throughput-test.sh - measure tokens per second

MODEL=${1:-"llama3.1:8b"}
PROMPT="Write a 500-word summary of enterprise AI adoption trends, covering security concerns, cost factors, and productivity benefits."
ITERATIONS=10

echo "=== Throughput Test: $MODEL ==="
echo "Iterations: $ITERATIONS"
echo ""

TOTAL_TOKENS=0
TOTAL_TIME=0

for i in $(seq 1 $ITERATIONS); do
  START=$(date +%s%N)
  
  RESPONSE=$(curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/generate \
    -d "{\"model\":\"$MODEL\",\"prompt\":\"$PROMPT\",\"stream\":false}")
  
  END=$(date +%s%N)
  ELAPSED=$(( (END - START) / 1000000 ))  # milliseconds
  
  TOKENS=$(echo $RESPONSE | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('eval_count', 0))")
  TPS=$(echo "scale=1; $TOKENS * 1000 / $ELAPSED" | bc)
  
  echo "Run $i: ${TOKENS} tokens in ${ELAPSED}ms = ${TPS} tok/s"
  
  TOTAL_TOKENS=$((TOTAL_TOKENS + TOKENS))
  TOTAL_TIME=$((TOTAL_TIME + ELAPSED))
done

AVG_TPS=$(echo "scale=1; $TOTAL_TOKENS * 1000 / $TOTAL_TIME" | bc)
echo ""
echo "Average: ${AVG_TPS} tokens/second"
echo "Total tokens generated: $TOTAL_TOKENS"
```

## Benchmark Suite 2: RAG Accuracy Testing

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# rag-accuracy-test.py

import json
import requests
from typing import List, Dict

# Test dataset: question/answer pairs from known document
# Create these from a document the client provides
TEST_CASES = [
    {
        "question": "What is the maximum annual deductible for the standard health plan?",
        "expected_keywords": ["$2,500", "deductible", "annual"],
        "document": "employee-benefits-2024.pdf"
    },
    {
        "question": "How many days of notice are required for contract termination?",
        "expected_keywords": ["30 days", "written notice", "termination"],
        "document": "vendor-contract-template.pdf"
    },
    # Add 10-20 test cases from client's actual documents
]

def evaluate_response(response: str, expected_keywords: List[str]) -> Dict:
    response_lower = response.lower()
    matched = [kw for kw in expected_keywords if kw.lower() in response_lower]
    score = len(matched) / len(expected_keywords)
    return {
        "score": score,
        "matched": matched,
        "missed": [kw for kw in expected_keywords if kw.lower() not in response_lower]
    }

def run_rag_benchmark():
    results = []
    
    for test in TEST_CASES:
        response = requests.post(
            "http://localhost:11434/api/generate",
            json={
                "model": "llama3.1:8b",
                "prompt": f"Based on the provided documents, answer: {test['question']}",
                "stream": False
            }
        ).json()
        
        eval_result = evaluate_response(
            response.get("response", ""),
            test["expected_keywords"]
        )
        
        results.append({
            "question": test["question"],
            "score": eval_result["score"],
            "passed": eval_result["score"] >= 0.8
        })
        
        print(f"Q: {test['question'][:60]}...")
        print(f"Score: {eval_result['score']:.0%} | Passed: {eval_result['passed']}")
        print()
    
    avg_score = sum(r["score"] for r in results) / len(results)
    pass_rate = sum(1 for r in results if r["passed"]) / len(results)
    
    print(f"=== BENCHMARK RESULTS ===")
    print(f"Average accuracy: {avg_score:.1%}")
    print(f"Pass rate (>80%): {pass_rate:.1%}")
    print(f"Cases tested: {len(results)}")
    
    return results

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_rag_benchmark()
```

## Benchmark Suite 3: Latency Under Load

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# concurrent-load-test.sh
# Simulates multiple users querying simultaneously

CONCURRENCY=${1:-5}  # Number of concurrent requests
MODEL="llama3.1:8b"
PROMPT="Summarize the key points in three sentences."

echo "=== Concurrency Test: $CONCURRENCY users ==="

# Launch concurrent requests and collect timing
for i in $(seq 1 $CONCURRENCY); do
  (
    START=$(date +%s%N)
    curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/generate \
      -d "{\"model\":\"$MODEL\",\"prompt\":\"$PROMPT User $i: describe enterprise AI\",\"stream\":false}" \
      > /dev/null
    END=$(date +%s%N)
    echo "User $i: $(( (END - START) / 1000000 ))ms"
  ) &
done

wait
echo "All requests complete"
```

## Benchmark Report Template

```markdown
# Private AI Performance Benchmark Report
**Client:** [Client Name]
**Date:** [Date]
**Hardware:** [Server specs]
**Model:** [Model name and version]

## Executive Summary
The private AI system achieved [X] tokens/second throughput and [X]% answer accuracy on client-specific documents, meeting/exceeding the performance targets established for this engagement.

## Throughput Results
| Test | Result | Target | Status |
|------|--------|--------|--------|
| Avg tokens/sec | [X] tok/s | >20 tok/s | PASS/FAIL |
| P95 response time | [X]ms | <15s | PASS/FAIL |
| Concurrent users (5) | [X]ms avg | <30s | PASS/FAIL |

## Accuracy Results  
| Test Suite | Score | Target | Status |
|-----------|-------|--------|--------|
| Document Q&A | [X]% | >85% | PASS/FAIL |
| Fact extraction | [X]% | >90% | PASS/FAIL |
| Summarization quality | [X]/5 | >4/5 | PASS/FAIL |

## Cloud Comparison
| Metric | Private AI | Cloud GPT-4o | Delta |
|--------|-----------|--------------|-------|
| Response time | [X]s | [X]s | [+/-]s |
| Answer accuracy | [X]% | [X]% | [+/-]% |
| Cost per 1M queries | $[X] | $[X] | $[X] savings |

## Recommendation
[Pass/Fail with specific conditions]
```

## Typical Benchmark Results by Hardware

```
Consumer GPU (RTX 4090, 24GB):
  Llama 3.1 8B:  45-65 tokens/sec
  Llama 3.1 70B: N/A (doesn't fit)
  Concurrent users: 2-3 before degradation

Professional GPU (A100 40GB):
  Llama 3.1 8B:  120-180 tokens/sec
  Llama 3.1 70B-Q4: 15-25 tokens/sec
  Concurrent users: 8-12 before degradation

Dual A100 80GB:
  Llama 3.1 8B:  200+ tokens/sec
  Llama 3.1 70B: 40-60 tokens/sec
  Concurrent users: 20-30 before degradation

CPU-only (32-core server, 256GB RAM):
  Llama 3.1 8B:  8-15 tokens/sec
  Suitable for: low-concurrency, overnight batch
  Not suitable for: interactive real-time use
```

title: Private AI Compliance Mapping: HIPAA, GDPR, SOC2, and FINRA
content:
A reference guide mapping private AI deployment controls to major regulatory compliance frameworks. Use this during sales to confirm the system meets the client's compliance obligations without lengthy legal review.

## HIPAA Compliance Mapping

HIPAA applies to covered entities (healthcare providers, plans, clearinghouses) and business associates processing Protected Health Information (PHI).

### Key HIPAA Requirements and Private AI Controls

| HIPAA Requirement | Reference | Private AI Control |
|-------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Limit PHI disclosure to minimum necessary | §164.502(b) | System processes only documents you ingest; no extraneous data sharing |
| Business Associate Agreement (BAA) | §164.308(b) | No BAA needed — you are not disclosing to a third party |
| Technical safeguards | §164.312 | TLS in transit, AES-256 at rest, RBAC via Open WebUI |
| Audit controls | §164.312(b) | Full query logging to PostgreSQL with user ID, timestamp, documents accessed |
| Transmission security | §164.312(e) | All traffic on internal network; no internet transmission |
| Access control | §164.312(a)(1) | Role-based access, session management, MFA via SSO integration |
| Facility access | §164.310(a) | Data center/server room physical security (client's responsibility) |

### Critical HIPAA Point for Sales
"When you use a cloud AI service to process PHI, that vendor becomes your Business Associate. You need a BAA, and that BAA must specifically cover AI processing — most early enterprise AI agreements did not. With private AI on your infrastructure, there is no Business Associate at all. PHI stays with you and only you."

### HIPAA Breach Notification Avoidance
If a cloud AI vendor suffers a breach affecting your PHI, you are obligated to notify HHS and affected individuals within 60 days (HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR §§ 164.400-414). A breach of your on-premises AI system is yours to manage, with no mandatory disclosure to a vendor's breach timeline.

## GDPR Compliance Mapping

GDPR applies to organizations processing personal data of EU residents, regardless of where the organization is located.

| GDPR Requirement | Article | Private AI Control |
|-----------------|---------|-------------------|
| Lawfulness of processing | Art. 6 | Processing occurs within your infrastructure; lawful basis established by your existing policies |
| Data minimization | Art. 5(1)(c) | You control what documents are ingested; no automatic collection |
| Storage limitation | Art. 5(1)(e) | PostgreSQL retention policies configurable; right-to-erasure via document deletion |
| Data transfers to third countries | Art. 44-49 | No transfers occur — data stays on-premises or in designated EU infrastructure |
| Records of processing activities | Art. 30 | Query audit logs provide complete processing record |
| Data breach notification | Art. 33 | Breach perimeter is your infrastructure; no vendor notifications required |
| Right to erasure | Art. 17 | DELETE query from knowledge_base WHERE id = [document_id] |
| Data processor agreements | Art. 28 | Not required — you are both controller and processor |

### GDPR Data Subject Rights Implementation
```sql
-- Right to access: find all content referencing a person
SELECT title, source, created_at
FROM knowledge_base
WHERE content ILIKE '%[person name]%'
  OR metadata->>'author' = '[person name]';

-- Right to erasure: delete specific document and its chunks
DELETE FROM document_chunks WHERE document_id = '[uuid]';
DELETE FROM knowledge_base WHERE id = '[uuid]';
-- Note: Also delete from vector index (handled by CASCADE)

-- Right to portability: export person's data
COPY (SELECT * FROM knowledge_base WHERE metadata->>'owner' = '[person]')
TO '/tmp/data-export.csv' CSV HEADER;
```

## SOC 2 Type II Alignment

SOC 2 evaluates against five Trust Services Criteria: Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy.

| Trust Service Criteria | Private AI Control |
|----------------------|-------------------|
| CC6.1 Logical access controls | Open WebUI RBAC, SSO integration, session timeouts |
| CC6.2 Prior to issuance of system credentials | Admin-managed user provisioning, no self-registration |
| CC6.3 Remove access | User deletion in Open WebUI, immediate access revocation |
| CC7.2 Monitor system components | Prometheus + Grafana monitoring, Loki log aggregation |
| CC7.3 Evaluate security events | Alertmanager alerts on anomalies, audit log review |
| A1.1 Availability commitments | Docker Compose healthchecks, automatic restart |
| A1.2 Environmental threats | Backup and DR runbook, documented RPO/RTO |
| C1.1 Confidentiality commitments | Internal-only network, no external data sharing |
| PI1.1 Processing integrity | Audit log captures all queries and responses |

## FINRA / Financial Services Compliance

| Requirement | Rule | Private AI Control |
|------------|------|-------------------|
| Books and records | FINRA Rule 4511 | All AI interactions logged to PostgreSQL, exportable |
| Supervision | FINRA Rule 3110 | Supervisory review possible via audit log queries |
| Data security | SEC Reg S-P | Data never leaves controlled infrastructure |
| Written supervisory procedures | FINRA Rule 3110(b) | Private AI deployment documented in WSPs |
| Electronic communications | FINRA Rule 4511 | AI-generated communications logged if used in client communications |

## Compliance Documentation Package

For clients requiring compliance documentation, provide:

1. **System Architecture Diagram** showing data flows (no external connections)
2. **Audit Log Schema** showing what is captured per query
3. **Access Control Matrix** showing user roles and permissions
4. **Encryption Standards** document (TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest)
5. **Backup and Recovery** runbook with RPO/RTO documentation
6. **Incident Response** procedure for AI system security events
7. **Network Diagram** showing system isolation from internet

These seven documents satisfy the documentation requirements for most regulatory audits without requiring the auditor to understand the underlying technology.

title: Document Ingestion Pipeline for Enterprise Knowledge Bases
Private AI Change Management and Adoption Playbook
content:
A structured change management approach for enterprise private AI rollouts, covering stakeholder engagement, pilot design, adoption metrics, and resistance management. The technology is rarely the hardest part — adoption is.

## The Adoption Challenge

Enterprise AI deployments fail most often not because the technology doesn't work, but because:
1. Employees don't understand what it does or how to use it effectively
2. Managers don't encourage usage or integrate it into workflows
3. The system is deployed but no one is responsible for curating the knowledge base
4. Early bad experiences (hallucinations, slow responses) create lasting negative impressions
5. No one measures adoption or success, so no one improves it

This playbook addresses each failure mode with specific interventions.

## Phase 1: Stakeholder Alignment (Weeks -4 to -2)

### Sponsor Identification
Private AI deployments need three types of sponsors:

1. **Executive Sponsor** (C-suite): Provides budget, sends org-wide communication, signals this matters
2. **IT Champion** (CTO/IT Director): Owns technical deployment, trusted on security questions
3. **Business Champions** (2-3 department heads): Validate use cases, become internal evangelists

Without all three, adoption will stall.

### Stakeholder Workshop Agenda (2 hours)
```
Hour 1: What and Why
  - 15 min: Live demo of the system with their specific documents
  - 20 min: Data privacy and security walkthrough (for CISO/legal)
  - 15 min: ROI and cost analysis review
  - 10 min: Questions

Hour 2: How We'll Do This
  - 20 min: Pilot design (who, what use cases, success criteria)
  - 20 min: Change management plan review
  - 20 min: Roles and responsibilities
```

## Phase 2: Pilot Design (Week -1)

### Pilot User Selection Criteria
Do NOT select random users or volunteers for the pilot. Select strategically:

| Criteria | Why |
|----------|-----|
| 30% heavy AI users (currently using personal ChatGPT) | Will appreciate the upgrade; provide quality comparison |
| 30% skeptics who said "I don't need AI" | Converting them proves the value; they become credible advocates |
| 30% influential employees (team leads, respected peers) | Their adoption signals to others it's worth their time |
| 10% representatives of each department in scope | Ensures use cases discovered across the org |

**Pilot size:** 20-30 users is optimal. Enough for meaningful feedback, small enough to give each person attention.

### Pilot Success Criteria (agree before starting)
```
Quantitative:
- 70%+ of pilot users log in at least 3x per week by week 4
- Average session length > 5 minutes
- >80% accuracy on department-specific Q&A benchmark
- P95 response time < 20 seconds

Qualitative:
- 3+ testimonials from pilot users willing to share publicly
- 2+ concrete use cases documented with time savings
- Net Promoter Score > 30 among pilot group

Go/No-Go Decision: 2 of 3 quantitative targets + 1 qualitative = proceed to full rollout
```

## Phase 3: Knowledge Base Curation Strategy

The quality of the AI is directly determined by the quality of documents ingested. This is the most underestimated workload in any deployment.

### Knowledge Base Architect Role
Assign someone (part-time, ~5 hours/week) as KB Architect:
- Reviews document ingestion quality monthly
- Fields requests: "Can we add X to the KB?"
- Monitors query logs for common questions the KB can't answer
- Removes outdated documents when policies change
- Reports on KB health metrics

### Document Triage Criteria
```
Tier 1 - Must Ingest (high value, high frequency):
  - Employee handbook and HR policies
  - Standard operating procedures
  - Product/service documentation
  - Compliance and regulatory filings
  - Frequently asked internal questions and answers

Tier 2 - Should Ingest (moderate value):
  - Past project documentation
  - Vendor contracts and SOWs
  - Training materials
  - Meeting notes from recurring reviews

Tier 3 - Evaluate Before Ingesting:
  - Drafts and working documents (may create confusion)
  - Personal files (privacy considerations)
  - Outdated versions of Tier 1 documents (replace, don't add)
  - External news/articles (often too generic to be useful)
```

## Phase 4: Launch and Adoption Acceleration

### Week 1 Activation Sequence
```
Day 1 (Monday): 
  - Executive sponsor sends org-wide email (template below)
  - IT sends access credentials to all users
  - Helpdesk briefed on common questions

Day 2:
  - 30-minute group training session (record it)
  - "Quick wins" document distributed: 5 things to try today

Day 3-5:
  - Department champions host informal coffee chat / demo
  - Slack/Teams channel opened for questions and sharing
```

### Executive Launch Email Template
```
Subject: Introducing [Company Name]'s AI Assistant — Private and Secure

Team,

Starting today, all [team/department/company] employees have access to our new private AI assistant.

This is different from public tools like ChatGPT. All data stays on our servers — nothing leaves our network. You can use it with actual company documents, client information, and proprietary materials.

To get started: [URL]
Training recording: [URL]
For questions: [Slack channel or email]

Over the next 30 days, we'll be measuring how this changes how we work. I encourage you to try it for [2-3 specific use cases relevant to this audience].

[Executive Sponsor Name]
```

## Phase 5: Measuring and Sustaining Adoption

### Monthly Adoption Dashboard
```sql
-- Active users last 30 days
SELECT 
  date_trunc('week', created_at) AS week,
  count(DISTINCT user_id) AS active_users,
  count(*) AS total_queries,
  avg(latency_ms) AS avg_latency_ms
FROM ai_audit_log
WHERE created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
GROUP BY week
ORDER BY week;

-- Top query topics (identify gaps)
SELECT query, count(*) as frequency
FROM ai_audit_log
WHERE documents_referenced = '{}'  -- queries with no document match
GROUP BY query
ORDER BY frequency DESC
LIMIT 20;
-- These are questions users asked but KB couldn't answer → add these docs
```

### Resistance Management

| Resistance Type | Root Cause | Intervention |
|----------------|------------|-------------|
| "I tried it once, it gave a wrong answer" | Single bad experience generalized | Show the correct answer technique; explain when to verify |
| "I don't have time to learn a new tool" | Workflow disruption concern | Show 2-minute time savings example from their specific role |
| "I prefer to just search the intranet" | Familiarity with existing tool | Side-by-side comparison: 5 searches vs 1 AI query for same answer |
| "My manager doesn't use it" | Social proof missing | Engage manager directly; provide manager-specific use cases |
| "I'm worried about job security" | Underlying fear, rarely stated | Address directly: this handles the lookup work, not the judgment work |

title: GPU Infrastructure Planning for Enterprise Private AI Scale
content:
A decision framework for GPU infrastructure planning in enterprise private AI deployments, covering procurement options, capacity planning, and total cost modeling from 10 to 1,000+ concurrent users.

## The GPU Decision Framework

Three variables determine your GPU strategy:
1. **Concurrent users** - how many people query simultaneously during peak
2. **Model size** - larger models need more VRAM and are slower
3. **Latency tolerance** - interactive use vs batch processing

## Capacity Planning Model

### Throughput Estimation
```
Given:
  Users: N concurrent
  Average query: 500 tokens output
  Acceptable wait: 30 seconds

Required throughput:
  N users × 500 tokens / 30 seconds = X tokens/second needed

Examples:
  5 concurrent users:  5 × 500 / 30 = 83 tok/s needed
  10 concurrent users: 10 × 500 / 30 = 167 tok/s needed
  25 concurrent users: 25 × 500 / 30 = 417 tok/s needed
  50 concurrent users: 50 × 500 / 30 = 833 tok/s needed
```

### GPU Performance Reference
```
Consumer GPUs:
  RTX 3090 (24GB):  ~35 tok/s (Llama 3.1 8B)
  RTX 4090 (24GB):  ~65 tok/s (Llama 3.1 8B)
  Cost: $1,500-2,500

Professional/Workstation GPUs:
  RTX A6000 (48GB): ~80 tok/s (Llama 3.1 8B)
  Cost: $5,000-8,000

Data Center GPUs:
  A100 40GB:   ~150 tok/s (Llama 3.1 8B)
  A100 80GB:   ~180 tok/s (Llama 3.1 8B)
  H100 80GB:   ~400 tok/s (Llama 3.1 8B)
  H100 SXM:    ~600 tok/s (Llama 3.1 8B)
  Cost: $15,000-40,000 each
```

### Sizing Table by Organization Size

| Org Size | AI Users | Peak Concurrent | Recommended GPU | Servers | Total GPU Cost |
|----------|----------|----------------|-----------------|---------|---------------|
| Startup (50 emp) | 15 | 3-5 | 2× RTX 4090 | 1 | $5,000 |
| Small (200 emp) | 60 | 10-15 | 2× A100 40GB | 1 | $30,000 |
| Medium (500 emp) | 150 | 25-35 | 4× A100 80GB | 2 | $80,000 |
| Large (2000 emp) | 600 | 80-100 | 8× H100 | 4 | $320,000 |
| Enterprise (5000+) | 1500+ | 200+ | H100 cluster | 8-16 | $800K+ |

## Procurement Options

### Option A: Purchase Outright
- Full control, no ongoing hardware costs
- Amortize over 4-5 years
- Best for: organizations with CapEx budget, long-term commitment
- Risk: GPU technology changes rapidly; H100 purchased today may feel obsolete in 3 years

### Option B: Server Leasing / DaaS
- Operating expense, no large upfront
- Refresh hardware every 2-3 years automatically
- Best for: organizations preferring OpEx, uncertain long-term AI needs
- Cost: typically 30-40% premium over 3 years vs purchase

### Option C: On-Demand Cloud GPU (Burst)
```bash
# Use cloud GPU for overflow demand
# While keeping sensitive data processing local

# Architecture: local for sensitive queries, cloud burst for non-sensitive
# Lambda Labs: H100 at $2.49/hour
# RunPod: A100 at $1.09/hour
# Vast.ai: Various GPUs from $0.30/hour

# IMPORTANT: Cloud burst only for NON-SENSITIVE workloads
# Classification, summarization of public documents, etc.
# PHI, PII, proprietary data: LOCAL ONLY
```

### Option D: Colocation
- Buy your own GPUs, house them in a data center
- Good for: multi-site orgs, redundancy requirements
- Cost: ~$1,000-2,000/month per rack + power at $0.08-0.12/kWh

## VRAM Planning for Multi-Model Deployment

```
Multiple models can share a GPU server if VRAM allows:

Example: 80GB A100 running:
  Llama 3.1 8B (primary):     10GB VRAM
  Nomic embed-text:            1GB VRAM
  Phi-3 Mini (fast responses): 4GB VRAM
  Buffer (inference peaks):   15GB VRAM
  ─────────────────────────────────────
  Total used:                 30GB / 80GB
  Remaining for burst:        50GB (could load 70B model temporarily)

Ollama manages this automatically with OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS
```

## Energy and Cooling Considerations

```
Power consumption by GPU:
  RTX 4090:  450W TDP → ~350W typical
  A100 80GB: 400W TDP → ~320W typical
  H100 SXM:  700W TDP → ~550W typical

Cooling requirement:
  1kW compute = ~3,413 BTU/hr cooling
  Standard server rack: 5-10kW
  GPU server rack:     20-40kW
  → Requires dedicated datacenter cooling
  → Most office environments cannot handle this

For office deployments (RTX 4090-class):
  Single card: ~350W, manageable with good airflow
  Two cards:   ~700W, need dedicated circuit
  Four cards:  ~1,400W, borderline for office, needs IT room
```

## Hardware Procurement Timeline

```
For projects in 2025-2026:
  A100 supply:  Generally available, 4-8 week lead time
  H100 supply:  High demand, 8-16 week lead time, allocations required
  RTX 4090:    Consumer availability, 1-2 week
  
Planning recommendation:
  Start hardware procurement 3 months before deployment target
  Have 20% spare capacity at launch (growth absorber)
  Plan GPU refresh at year 3-4 for cost-effective performance upgrade
```
